As you read, you learn what has been investigated, what methods have been used, what findings have emerged, and - crucially - what questions remain unanswered. These unanswered questions become candidates for your own research.

The combination of concept mapping and literature immersion is powerful because they complement each other. Concept mapping helps you generate possibilities; literature immersion helps you evaluate which of those possibilities are most viable and most needed. Together, they move you efficiently from a broad interest to a focused, original, and significant research question.

Several practical tools can assist with the narrowing process. Concept mapping involves writing your broad topic in the centre of a page and branching outward with related subtopics, variables, populations, and contexts. This visual technique helps you see the full landscape of possibilities and identify the most promising avenues for investigation. Mind mapping serves a similar function but emphasises hierarchical relationships between ideas (Novak & Cañas, 2008).

Perhaps the most powerful tool for narrowing is immersion in the literature. As you read existing studies on your broad topic, you will discover what has already been investigated, what methods have been used, what findings have been reported, and - most importantly - what remains unknown. The gaps, contradictions, and unanswered questions you encounter in the literature are the raw material from which your research question will be formed. A literature review is not just a requirement of the research process; it is the mechanism through which a broad curiosity is transformed into a precise inquiry.

6.3 From Question to Problem Statement

Once you have a focused question, you need to frame it as a research problem. A problem statement is a formal academic argument for why your question needs to be answered. It typically includes three elements: what is known, what is not known, and why the gap matters.

The problem statement is your pitch to the academic community. It says: "Here is an important issue. Here is what previous research has established. Here is what we still do not know. And here is why filling this gap would be valuable." A strong problem statement makes the reader nod and think "Yes, that question does need to be answered."

Writing a problem statement forces you to articulate the significance of your research in concrete terms. It is not enough to say "Little research has been done on X" - you must also explain why research on X matters. What decisions will be better informed? What theories will be refined? What practices will be improved? The answers to these questions transform your problem statement from a description of a gap into a compelling case for investigation.

Once you have a focused question, you need to frame it as a research problem. A problem statement is a formal academic argument for why your question needs to be answered. It typically includes three elements: what is known, what is not known, and why the gap matters.

The problem statement is your pitch to the academic community. It says: "Here is an important issue. Here is what previous research has established. Here is what we still do not know. And here is why filling this gap would be valuable." A strong problem statement makes the reader nod and think "Yes, that question does need to be answered."

Writing a problem statement forces you to articulate the significance of your research in concrete terms. It is not enough to say "Little research has been done on X" - you must also explain why research on X matters. What decisions will be better informed? What theories will be refined? What practices will be improved? The answers to these questions transform your problem statement from a description of a gap into a compelling case for investigation.

Once you have a focused question, you need to frame it as a research problem. A problem statement is a formal academic argument for why your question needs to be answered. It typically includes three elements: what is known, what is not known, and why the gap matters.

The problem statement is your pitch to the academic community. It says: "Here is an important issue. Here is what previous research has established. Here is what we still do not know. And here is why filling this gap would be valuable." A strong problem statement makes the reader nod and think "Yes, that question does need to be answered."

Writing a problem statement forces you to articulate the significance of your research in concrete terms. It is not enough to say "Little research has been done on X" - you must also explain why research on X matters. What decisions will be better informed? What theories will be refined? What practices will be improved? The answers to these questions transform your problem statement from a description of a gap into a compelling case for investigation.

Once a focused research question has been formulated, the researcher must articulate it within a problem statement - a concise declaration of the specific issue the study addresses, why it matters, and what the study aims to accomplish. A strong problem statement typically includes three elements: a description of the broader context and its significance, identification of the specific gap or problem, and a statement of the study's purpose and expected contribution. The problem statement bridges the gap between the general importance of the topic and the specific focus of the study, providing the reader with a clear rationale for why this research needs to be done (Creswell & Creswell, 2018).

Chapter Summary

Moving from a broad interest to a focused research question is one of the most challenging and important skills in research. This chapter has presented systematic strategies for narrowing, including concept mapping, mind mapping, and literature immersion. The process culminates in a clear problem statement that articulates the gap, the significance, and the purpose of the study.

Key Terms

Concept map - A visual tool for organising and exploring relationships between ideas, used to narrow broad topics.

Problem statement - A concise articulation of the specific issue a study addresses, its significance, and the study's purpose.

Literature immersion - The process of extensively reading existing research to identify gaps, patterns, and opportunities for new inquiry.

Review Questions

1. Describe the step-by-step process of narrowing a broad topic into a focused research question.

2. Create a concept map for a broad topic of your choice, showing how it can be narrowed along multiple dimensions.

3. What is a problem statement, and how does it differ from a research question? Write a sample problem statement for a topic in your field.

6.4 The Role of Supervisors and Mentors in Narrowing

While the process of narrowing from a broad interest to a focused question is ultimately your responsibility, supervisors and mentors play a crucial role in guiding this process. They bring experience, disciplinary knowledge, and a broader perspective that can help you avoid common mistakes and identify the most productive directions for your research.

A good supervisor will not simply tell you what question to ask. Instead, they will help you develop the skills to identify good questions yourself. They might suggest readings that illuminate important debates in your field. They might point out gaps in the literature that you have not yet discovered. They might challenge your assumptions or push you to consider alternative approaches. And they will almost certainly save you from pursuing dead ends that their experience allows them to recognise.

However, the supervisor-student relationship in question development is not without its tensions. Supervisors have their own research interests, theoretical commitments, and methodological preferences, which may influence the direction they suggest for your research. This influence is not necessarily problematic - in fact, alignment between your question and your supervisor's expertise can be highly productive. But it is important to ensure that the question you pursue is genuinely yours, not merely a reflection of your supervisor's agenda.

To get the most from your supervisor during the narrowing process, come to meetings prepared. Bring written summaries of what you have read, the questions you are considering, and the reasons for and against each option. Ask specific questions rather than vague ones: "I am considering focusing on either mobile learning or blended learning - which do you think would be more productive, and why?" is more useful than "I do not know what to study." Take notes during meetings and follow up on suggestions promptly. And be honest about your interests and concerns - a good supervisor will respect your intellectual autonomy even as they guide your development.

6.5 Documenting Your Narrowing Journey

The process of narrowing from broad interest to focused question is rarely linear, and it is easy to lose track of the intellectual journey that brought you to your final question. Documenting this journey is valuable for several reasons: it provides material for the rationale section of your proposal, it helps you explain your research to others, and it creates a record that can be revisited if you need to reconsider your direction.

A research journal or research log is an excellent tool for this purpose. Record your initial interests, the readings that shaped your thinking, the questions you considered and rejected, and the reasons for each decision. Include not just your intellectual reasoning but also your emotional responses - the excitement you felt when you discovered a particular gap, the frustration when a promising direction turned out to be a dead end, the satisfaction when your question finally crystallised into a clear and compelling form. (Ortlipp, 2008)

This documentation also serves a pedagogical purpose. By reviewing your narrowing journey, you can identify patterns in your thinking that might be helpful or limiting. Do you tend to jump too quickly from broad interest to specific question, without sufficient exploration of the landscape? Or do you tend to explore endlessly without committing to a direction? Understanding your own tendencies allows you to compensate for them, deliberately slowing down if you tend to rush, or deliberately setting deadlines if you tend to procrastinate.

6.6 When Narrowing Goes Wrong

The narrowing process does not always proceed smoothly. Sometimes you narrow in the wrong direction, pursuing a question that turns out to be unanswerable, unoriginal, or uninteresting. Sometimes you narrow too far, producing a question so specific that the answer, whatever it might be, would have limited significance. And sometimes you cannot narrow at all, finding every direction equally appealing or equally problematic.

When narrowing goes wrong, the most important thing is to recognise the problem early and respond appropriately. If your question is unanswerable with available methods, consider modifying it to address a different aspect of the same phenomenon that is more amenable to investigation. If your question is unoriginal, consider what you could add - a new context, a new method, a new population - that would make it distinctive. If your question is too narrow, step back and ask what broader question it contributes to, then consider whether a slightly broader version might be more productive.

And if you are stuck, unable to narrow at all, try these strategies. First, impose artificial constraints: choose a specific population, a specific time frame, or a specific variable, and see where that leads. Second, talk to other people - supervisors, peers, practitioners in the field - and pay attention to the questions that emerge from conversation. Third, write about your dilemma: putting your struggle into words often reveals the source of the difficulty and suggests a way forward. And fourth, give yourself permission to make a provisional choice, knowing that you can revise it later. Often, the act of committing to a direction - even tentatively - generates the momentum needed to move forward.

6.7 Case Studies in Successful Narrowing

To illustrate the narrowing process in practice, consider the following examples of how real researchers moved from broad interests to focused, productive research questions.

Dr Abena Mensah began her doctoral research with a broad interest in "education in Ghana." Through literature immersion, she discovered that while primary education had been studied extensively, relatively little research existed on the experiences of mature students returning to university after career breaks. She narrowed further by focusing on a specific population (women aged 30-45 returning to university) and a specific aspect of their experience (the challenges of balancing academic demands with family responsibilities). Her final question - "How do Ghanaian women aged 30-45 experience and manage the competing demands of family caregiving and academic study during their first year of return to university?" - was specific enough to investigate through a series of in-depth interviews, yet broad enough to contribute to both educational research and gender studies.

Professor Kwame Asante began with an interest in "technology and learning." He narrowed this first to "mobile technology in higher education," then to "the use of WhatsApp for academic communication among university students." His literature review revealed that while several studies had examined WhatsApp use for social purposes, very few had investigated its role in academic study groups. He narrowed further to focus on a specific academic context (science courses) and a specific outcome (collaborative problem-solving). His final question - "Does participation in WhatsApp-based study groups improve collaborative problem-solving performance in undergraduate chemistry courses?" - was specific enough for a quasi-experimental study while addressing a practical question that many educators were asking.

These examples illustrate several key principles of successful narrowing. Both researchers began with genuine personal interest in a broad topic. Both used literature immersion to identify gaps and opportunities. Both narrowed progressively, through several stages, rather than jumping directly from broad topic to specific question. And both ended with questions that were clear, researchable, significant, and feasible - questions that led to productive and well-received research projects.

6.8 Practical Exercises for Narrowing Your Focus

The following exercises are designed to help you move from broad interest to focused question. They can be completed individually or with a peer group, and they draw on the strategies discussed throughout this chapter.

Exercise 1: The Five Whys. Start with your broad topic and ask yourself "why is this interesting or important?" Write down your answer. Then ask "why?" about that answer. Repeat five times. By the fifth iteration, you will have moved from a general interest to a much more specific and personally meaningful aspect of your topic. (Ohno, 1988)

Exercise 2: The Newspaper Test. Imagine that your study has been completed and the results are reported in a newspaper headline. What would the headline say? If you cannot write a clear, specific headline, your question may need to be narrowed further. The headline exercise forces you to distill your question to its essential elements and to think about what finding would be newsworthy.

Exercise 3: The Dinner Party Explanation. Explain your research question to someone outside academia - a family member, a friend, or a colleague in a different field. If they look confused or ask "but what exactly are you trying to find out?" your question may be too vague or too broadly framed. Their questions and reactions can help you identify which aspects of your research are most interesting and comprehensible to a non-specialist audience.

Exercise 4: The Variable Matrix. List all the variables that might be relevant to your topic. Then create a matrix showing the relationships between them. Identify which relationships have been studied extensively, which have been studied in some contexts but not others, and which have not been studied at all. This matrix reveals opportunities for original research and helps you identify the specific relationship your study will investigate.

Exercise 5: The Feasibility Checklist. For your current research question, answer these questions: Do I have access to the population I want to study? Can I collect the data I need within my time frame? Do I have the analytical skills required? Will I be able to get ethical approval? Can I fund the study? If the answer to any of these questions is no, consider how you might modify your question to make it more feasible without sacrificing its significance.

chapters.title: Chapter 7: Testing Your Question: Feasibility, Originality, and Significance
chapters.part: PART TWO: Asking the Right Questions
chapters.content:
Chapter 7

Testing Your Question: Feasibility, Originality, and Significance

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Apply systematic criteria to evaluate the quality of a research question
• Assess the feasibility of a proposed study given available resources
• Determine whether a research question is sufficiently original to warrant investigation
• Evaluate the significance of a research question for theory, practice, and policy
• Use diagnostic tools to refine and strengthen your research question

7.1 Why Testing Your Question Matters

Before investing months or years in a research project, you need to test whether your question can actually be investigated. Many promising questions fail one or more of the tests described in this chapter, and it is far better to discover this early than after you have collected data or written three chapters of your thesis.

The FINER framework provides a systematic way to evaluate your research question across five dimensions: Feasibility, Interest, Novelty, Ethics, and Relevance. A question that passes all five tests has a strong chance of leading to a successful and meaningful study.

Figure 7.1 The FINER framework for testing research questions.

Feasibility asks whether the study can actually be conducted within the constraints of time, budget, expertise, and access. A brilliant question that would require ten years and a million-dollar budget is not feasible for a masters thesis. Be honest with yourself about what you can realistically accomplish.

Interest asks whether the question engages you and the scholarly community. A question that bores you will lead to a painful research experience, no matter how important it may be objectively. You will be living with this question for months or years - make sure it is one you genuinely want to answer.

Before investing months or years in a research project, you need to test whether your question can actually be investigated. Many promising questions fail one or more of the tests described in this chapter, and it is far better to discover this early than after you have collected data or written three chapters of your thesis.

The FINER framework provides a systematic way to evaluate your research question across five dimensions: Feasibility, Interest, Novelty, Ethics, and Relevance. A question that passes all five tests has a strong chance of leading to a successful and meaningful study.

Figure 7.1 The FINER framework for testing research questions.

You will be living with this question for months or years - make sure it is one you genuinely want to answer.

Many promising questions fail one or more of the tests described in this chapter, and it is far better to discover this early than after you have collected data or written three chapters of your thesis.

The FINER framework provides a systematic way to evaluate your research question across five dimensions: Feasibility, Interest, Novelty, Ethics, and Relevance. A question that passes all five tests has a strong chance of leading to a successful and meaningful study.

Figure 7.1 The FINER framework for testing research questions.

Feasibility asks whether the study can actually be conducted within the constraints of time, budget, expertise, and access. A brilliant question that would require ten years and a million-dollar budget is not feasible for a masters thesis. Be honest with yourself about what you can realistically accomplish.

Interest asks whether the question engages you and the scholarly community. A question that bores you will lead to a painful research experience, no matter how important it may be objectively. You will be living with this question for months or years - make sure it is one you genuinely want to answer.

Formulating a research question is not enough - the question must be rigorously tested before significant time and resources are invested in the study. Many potentially interesting questions fail when subjected to critical scrutiny: some prove impossible to investigate with available methods; others turn out to have already been answered; and still others, while technically researchable, are too trivial to justify the effort. A systematic evaluation of your question before you begin can save months of wasted work and ensure that your study makes a genuine contribution to knowledge (Kumar, 2019).

7.2 Feasibility: Can This Study Actually Be Done?

The FINER Framework (Hulley, Cummings, Browner, Grady, & Newman, 2013) for Testing Research Questions

✅ Feasible

Can be done with available time, resources, and expertise.

💡 Interesting

Engages the researcher and the scholarly community.

🆕 Novel

Adds something new — confirms, refutes, or extends existing knowledge.

🤝 Ethical

Can be conducted without undue risk or ethical violations.

⭐ Relevant

Has implications for theory, policy, or practice.

Feasibility is the most practical of the five FINER criteria, and it is the one most frequently overlooked by enthusiastic but inexperienced researchers. A feasible study is one that can be completed within your available time, with your available resources, and within your current level of expertise.

Time is usually the biggest constraint. A doctoral study typically takes three to five years; a masters study, one to two years. But research always takes longer than you expect. Data collection is delayed by participant recruitment. Analysis reveals unexpected complexities. Your supervisor wants revisions. Build generous buffers into your timeline, and choose a question that can be investigated within your actual time frame, not your optimistic estimate.

Resources include funding, equipment, software, and access to participants or data. If your question requires specialised equipment you do not have, or access to a population that is difficult to reach, these practical obstacles may make your question infeasible regardless of its intellectual merit.

Feasibility is the most practical of the five FINER criteria, and it is the one most frequently overlooked by enthusiastic but inexperienced researchers. A feasible study is one that can be completed within your available time, with your available resources, and within your current level of expertise.

Resources include funding, equipment, software, and access to participants or data. If your question requires specialised equipment you do not have, or access to a population that is difficult to reach, these practical obstacles may make your question infeasible regardless of its intellectual merit.

A feasible study is one that can be completed within your available time, with your available resources, and within your current level of expertise.

Time is usually the biggest constraint. A doctoral study typically takes three to five years; a masters study, one to two years. But research always takes longer than you expect. Data collection is delayed by participant recruitment. Analysis reveals unexpected complexities. Your supervisor wants revisions. Build generous buffers into your timeline, and choose a question that can be investigated within your actual time frame, not your optimistic estimate.

Resources include funding, equipment, software, and access to participants or data. If your question requires specialised equipment you do not have, or access to a population that is difficult to reach, these practical obstacles may make your question infeasible regardless of its intellectual merit.

Feasibility refers to the practical possibility of conducting the study given the constraints of time, money, access, expertise, and ethical considerations. A question may be intellectually compelling but practically impossible to investigate. Can you access the population you want to study? Do you have the skills to use the required analytical methods? Can the study be completed within your available timeframe? Is the research ethically permissible? These practical considerations are not secondary - they are essential determinants of whether a study can be successfully completed (Bryman, 2016).

A useful exercise is to conduct a feasibility audit: list every resource you will need (data, participants, equipment, software, time, funding, ethical approval) and honestly assess whether each is available. Where gaps exist, consider whether they can be addressed through alternative designs, collaborations, or institutional support.

7.3 Originality: Does This Add Something New?

Originality does not mean that no one has ever thought about your topic before. It means that your specific question, investigated with your specific methods, in your specific context, will produce knowledge that did not previously exist. This is a much more achievable standard than most students realise.

Figure 7.2 Types of originality and their relative frequency in doctoral research.

New evidence means studying a question that has not been empirically investigated before. New method means applying a methodology that has not been used to study this particular phenomenon. New context means investigating a known phenomenon in a new population, setting, or culture. New synthesis means combining insights from multiple existing studies to produce a new understanding. And new theory means proposing or modifying a theoretical framework.

Originality does not mean that no one has ever thought about your topic before. It means that your specific question, investigated with your specific methods, in your specific context, will produce knowledge that did not previously exist. This is a much more achievable standard than most students realise.

Figure 7.2 Types of originality and their relative frequency in doctoral research.

New evidence means studying a question that has not been empirically investigated before. New method means applying a methodology that has not been used to study this particular phenomenon. New context means investigating a known phenomenon in a new population, setting, or culture. New synthesis means combining insights from multiple existing studies to produce a new understanding. And new theory means proposing or modifying a theoretical framework.

Originality does not mean that no one has ever thought about your topic before. It means that your specific question, investigated with your specific methods, in your specific context, will produce knowledge that did not previously exist. This is a much more achievable standard than most students realise.

Figure 7.2 Types of originality and their relative frequency in doctoral research.

New evidence means studying a question that has not been empirically investigated before. New method means applying a methodology that has not been used to study this particular phenomenon. New context means investigating a known phenomenon in a new population, setting, or culture. New synthesis means combining insights from multiple existing studies to produce a new understanding. And new theory means proposing or modifying a theoretical framework.

Originality does not mean that your research must be entirely unprecedented - very few studies are. Rather, originality means that your study adds something new to the existing body of knowledge. This might be a new question that has not been asked before, a new population or context that has not been studied, a new method applied to an existing question, or a new theoretical perspective brought to bear on an established phenomenon. The key is to demonstrate that your study goes beyond what is already known (Phillips & Pugh, 2015).

The literature review is the primary tool for establishing originality. By demonstrating comprehensive knowledge of what has already been done, you can clearly articulate what your study adds. Phrases such as "No previous study has examined..." or "While extensive research has explored X, the relationship between X and Y in the context of Z remains unexplored" signal originality to readers and reviewers.

7.4 Significance: Does It Matter?

A research question can be feasible and original but still lack significance - it may address a gap that nobody cares about. Significance means that the answer to the question will make a meaningful difference: it will advance theoretical understanding, inform practical decision-making, influence policy, or improve outcomes for a particular population. When evaluating significance, ask: Who will benefit from the answers to this question? What decisions or actions will be informed by the findings? How will the field be different as a result of this study? (Creswell & Creswell, 2018).

Chapter Summary

Every research question should be tested against three critical criteria: feasibility (can it be done?), originality (does it add something new?), and significance (does it matter?). This chapter has provided practical tools for conducting these evaluations, including feasibility audits, literature-based originality assessments, and significance evaluations tied to theory, practice, and policy.

Key Terms

Feasibility - The practical possibility of conducting a study given constraints of time, resources, access, and ethics.

Originality - The quality of contributing something new to existing knowledge through a novel question, method, context, or perspective.

Significance - The importance or meaningfulness of a research question for advancing theory, informing practice, or influencing policy.

Feasibility audit - A systematic assessment of all resources needed for a study and whether they are available.

Review Questions

1. Explain the three criteria for testing a research question. Why must all three be satisfied?

2. Conduct a feasibility audit for a research question you are considering. What challenges do you anticipate?

3. How can a researcher demonstrate that their study is original? What role does the literature review play in establishing originality?

4. Give an example of a research question that is feasible and original but lacks significance. How could it be modified to increase its significance?

7.4 Ethical Considerations in Research Design

Ethics is not an afterthought in research design - it is a fundamental consideration that must inform every aspect of your study, from question formulation to data collection to dissemination of findings. Ethical research protects the rights, dignity, and welfare of research participants, ensures the integrity of the research process, and maintains public trust in the scholarly enterprise.

The core principles of research ethics, as articulated in the Belmont Report and subsequent ethical frameworks, include respect for persons, beneficence, and justice. Respect for persons requires that participants give informed consent, that their autonomy is respected, and that those with diminished autonomy receive additional protections. Beneficence requires that the potential benefits of the research justify the potential risks, and that researchers take all reasonable steps to minimise harm. Justice requires that the burdens and benefits of research are distributed fairly, and that vulnerable populations are not exploited for the benefit of more privileged groups. (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects, 1979) (Beauchamp & Childress, 2019)

These principles have practical implications for your research design. If your study involves human participants, you will need to obtain ethics approval from an institutional review board (IRB) or ethics committee. This process requires you to describe your study in detail, explain how you will recruit participants, describe the potential risks and benefits, and demonstrate that you have adequate safeguards in place. The ethics review process can be time-consuming, and you should build it into your timeline from the beginning.

Particular care is needed when your research involves vulnerable populations - children, prisoners, people with cognitive impairments, refugees, or members of marginalised communities. These groups may face additional pressures to participate, may have difficulty understanding consent documents, or may be harmed in ways that are not immediately apparent. Working with vulnerable populations requires not just ethical approval but ethical sensitivity - an ongoing awareness of the power dynamics in the research relationship and a commitment to ensuring that your research serves the interests of participants as well as the interests of scholarship.

7.5 Pilot Testing and Feasibility Studies

Before committing fully to a research project, it is often valuable to conduct a pilot study or feasibility assessment. A pilot study is a small-scale preliminary investigation that tests your methods, instruments, and procedures before you invest the time and resources required for a full-scale study. Feasibility studies focus specifically on whether a planned study can be conducted as designed, examining issues such as participant recruitment, data collection logistics, and resource requirements. (Van Teijlingen & Hundley, 2001)

Pilot studies serve multiple purposes. They help you identify problems with your research instruments - questions that are ambiguous, response options that are inadequate, or interview protocols that are too long or too short. They help you estimate the time and resources needed for data collection. They provide preliminary data that can inform your sample size calculation. And they give you practice with your methods, building confidence and competence before the stakes are higher.

Common problems discovered through pilot testing include recruitment difficulties (fewer eligible or willing participants than expected), measurement problems (instruments that do not behave as expected in your specific context), and practical obstacles (scheduling difficulties, technology failures, access restrictions). Discovering these problems during a small pilot study is far preferable to discovering them after you have invested months in a full-scale investigation.

When reporting the results of a pilot study, be careful not to overinterpret the findings. Pilot studies are typically too small to produce statistically significant results, and their primary purpose is methodological rather than substantive. Focus your report on what you learned about the feasibility and adequacy of your methods, and describe the modifications you made in response to what you discovered.

7.6 Refining Your Question Through Iteration

The process of testing your research question is not a one-time event. It is an iterative process in which testing, reflection, and refinement alternate until you arrive at a question that is clear, focused, feasible, original, ethical, and significant. Most successful research questions go through multiple iterations before they reach their final form.

Each iteration should be documented and shared. Write down your question, note what concerns you have about it, and describe how you plan to address those concerns. Share your evolving question with your supervisor, peers, and other scholars in your field. Listen carefully to their feedback, not to accept it uncritically, but to consider perspectives and possibilities that you might have missed.

Be prepared for the possibility that testing reveals fundamental problems with your question - problems that require you to change direction significantly. This can be disheartening, especially if you have invested considerable time and emotional energy in a particular direction. But discovering a fundamental problem early is always better than discovering it late, and the knowledge you gained from your initial exploration will inform and enrich whatever direction you take next.

7.7 Resource Assessment for Research Projects

A realistic assessment of available resources is essential for determining the feasibility of your research project. Resources include not just funding but time, expertise, equipment, software, access to participants, and institutional support. Many promising research projects have been abandoned or compromised because the researcher underestimated the resources required or overestimated the resources available.

Time is typically the most critical and most underestimated resource. Research almost always takes longer than planned, and building generous time buffers into your schedule is essential. As a rule of thumb, multiply your initial time estimate by 1.5 to 2.0 to arrive at a more realistic projection. This buffer accounts for the unexpected delays that are inevitable in any research project: ethics approval taking longer than expected, participants being harder to recruit than anticipated, equipment malfunctioning, data analysis revealing unexpected complexities, and supervisors requesting revisions.

Access to participants or data sources is another critical resource that is often taken for granted. If your study requires interviewing senior executives, will you actually be able to secure their participation? If it requires data from government agencies, will they grant you access? If it requires a large sample of a specific population, do you have the networks and recruitment strategies needed to reach them? These questions should be answered before you commit to a research design, not after.

Technical expertise is a resource that is easy to overlook. If your study requires statistical techniques that you have not yet learned, factor in the time needed to acquire those skills. If it requires software that you are not familiar with, allow time for training. If it requires laboratory equipment that you have not used before, arrange for supervised practice before you begin your actual data collection.

Financial resources matter more for some studies than others. Large-scale surveys, experimental studies with multiple conditions, and research that involves travel or specialised equipment can be expensive. If your study requires significant funding, explore all available sources early: departmental research funds, external grants, scholarships, and collaborative arrangements with other researchers or institutions.

7.8 From Research Question to Research Proposal

The final step in the question-testing process is the development of a formal research proposal. The proposal is the document in which you present your research question, justify its importance, describe how you plan to investigate it, and demonstrate that the project is feasible and ethical. It is both a planning tool for yourself and a persuasive document for others - supervisors, ethics committees, and potential funders.

A well-structured research proposal typically includes several key sections. The introduction establishes the context for your research, identifies the gap in existing knowledge, and presents your research question. The literature review demonstrates your mastery of the relevant scholarship and builds the case for your study's contribution. The methodology section describes your research design, data collection methods, sampling strategy, and analytical approach. The timeline and resource plan demonstrates that the project is feasible within your available time and resources. And the ethics section addresses any ethical considerations and describes how you will protect the rights and welfare of research participants.

Writing the proposal is itself a valuable intellectual exercise, because it forces you to think through every aspect of your project before you begin. Many problems that would be difficult and expensive to solve during the research itself - unclear definitions, inappropriate methods, unrealistic timelines, ethical oversights - can be identified and addressed at the proposal stage, when the cost of making changes is minimal.

The proposal should also be seen as a living document that evolves as your research progresses. While significant changes to the research question or methodology after approval require formal amendment, minor refinements and adjustments are normal and expected. The proposal provides a framework for your research, not a straitjacket. Its purpose is to guide your investigation, not to prevent you from responding to unexpected discoveries or unforeseen challenges.

chapters.title: Chapter 8: Common Question Mistakes and How to Fix Them
chapters.part: PART TWO: Asking the Right Questions
chapters.content:
Chapter 8

Common Question Mistakes and How to Fix Them

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Identify the most common errors in research question formulation
• Diagnose problems in existing research questions
• Apply corrective strategies to transform weak questions into strong ones
• Recognise questions that are too broad, too narrow, or poorly structured
• Use peer feedback and self-assessment to improve question quality

8.1 Why Good Researchers Write Bad Questions

Common Research Question Mistakes
MistakeProblemFix
Too broad"What is the impact of technology on education?"Specify population, context, and outcome variable
Too narrow"Do left-handed students in Class 3A prefer blue pens?"Broaden to yield meaningful, generalisable insights
Value-laden"Why is traditional education better than online?"Remove assumptions; use neutral, objective language
Unanswerable"What will education look like in 100 years?"Focus on questions that can be tested with evidence
Double-barrelled"Does class size affect motivation and performance?"Split into two separate, focused questions

Even experienced researchers sometimes write poor research questions. This is not because they lack intelligence or training, but because question formulation is inherently difficult. It requires balancing multiple competing demands: the question must be broad enough to be interesting but narrow enough to be feasible; it must be specific enough to be researchable but flexible enough to accommodate unexpected findings.

The most common mistakes fall into recognisable patterns, and learning to identify these patterns in your own work is one of the most valuable skills you can develop as a researcher.

Figure 8.1 The six most common research question mistakes.

Common Research Question Mistakes
MistakeProblemFix
Too broad"What is the impact of technology on education?"Specify population, context, and outcome variable
Too narrow"Do left-handed students in Class 3A prefer blue pens?"Broaden to yield meaningful, generalisable insights
Value-laden"Why is traditional education better than online?"Remove assumptions; use neutral, objective language
Unanswerable"What will education look like in 100 years?"Focus on questions that can be tested with evidence
Double-barrelled"Does class size affect motivation and performance?"Split into two separate, focused questions

Even experienced researchers sometimes write poor research questions. This is not because they lack intelligence or training, but because question formulation is inherently difficult. It requires balancing multiple competing demands: the question must be broad enough to be interesting but narrow enough to be feasible; it must be specific enough to be researchable but flexible enough to accommodate unexpected findings.

The most common mistakes fall into recognisable patterns, and learning to identify these patterns in your own work is one of the most valuable skills you can develop as a researcher.

Figure 8.1 The six most common research question mistakes.

Even experienced researchers sometimes write poor research questions. This is not because they lack intelligence or training, but because question formulation is inherently difficult. It requires balancing multiple competing demands: the question must be broad enough to be interesting but narrow enough to be feasible; it must be specific enough to be researchable but flexible enough to accommodate unexpected findings.

The most common mistakes fall into recognisable patterns, and learning to identify these patterns in your own work is one of the most valuable skills you can develop as a researcher.

Figure 8.1 The six most common research question mistakes.

Common Research Question Mistakes
MistakeProblemFix
Too broad"What is the impact of technology on education?"Specify population, context, and outcome variable
Too narrow"Do left-handed students in Class 3A prefer blue pens?"Broaden to yield meaningful, generalisable insights
Value-laden"Why is traditional education better than online?"Remove assumptions; use neutral, objective language
Unanswerable"What will education look like in 100 years?"Focus on questions that can be tested with evidence
Double-barrelled"Does class size affect motivation and performance?"Split into two separate, focused questions

Even experienced researchers sometimes formulate weak research questions. The reasons are varied: insufficient engagement with the literature, pressure to begin data collection before the question is fully developed, unfamiliarity with the conventions of question writing in a particular field, or simple lack of feedback. For students and early-career researchers, the most common cause is impatience - the desire to move forward with the study before the intellectual groundwork has been properly laid. This chapter examines the most frequent question mistakes and provides practical strategies for correcting them (Kumar, 2019).

8.2 Questions That Are Too Broad

A question that is too broad tries to cover too much ground. "What is the impact of technology on education?" is so broad that it could generate thousands of different studies, each investigating a different aspect of the relationship. The problem is not that the question is uninteresting - it is that it is unmanageable. No single study can answer it.

The fix for a too-broad question is to specify the population, the context, the variables, and the relationship you want to investigate. "What is the effect of tablet-based reading programmes on literacy scores among Year 3 students in rural Ghanaian schools?" is a narrowed version of the same interest, but it is now specific enough to be investigated by a single study.

A question that is too broad tries to cover too much ground. "What is the impact of technology on education?" is so broad that it could generate thousands of different studies, each investigating a different aspect of the relationship. The problem is not that the question is uninteresting - it is that it is unmanageable. No single study can answer it.

The fix for a too-broad question is to specify the population, the context, the variables, and the relationship you want to investigate. "What is the effect of tablet-based reading programmes on literacy scores among Year 3 students in rural Ghanaian schools?" is a narrowed version of the same interest, but it is now specific enough to be investigated by a single study.

A question that is too broad tries to cover too much ground. "What is the impact of technology on education?" is so broad that it could generate thousands of different studies, each investigating a different aspect of the relationship. The problem is not that the question is uninteresting - it is that it is unmanageable. No single study can answer it.

The fix for a too-broad question is to specify the population, the context, the variables, and the relationship you want to investigate. "What is the effect of tablet-based reading programmes on literacy scores among Year 3 students in rural Ghanaian schools?" is a narrowed version of the same interest, but it is now specific enough to be investigated by a single study.

The most common mistake is formulating a question that is too broad to be answered by a single study. "What is the impact of globalisation on developing countries?" is impossibly broad - it could be the subject of hundreds of studies across dozens of disciplines. The fix is progressive narrowing: specify the aspect of globalisation, the type of impact, the particular country or region, and the population of interest. "How has trade liberalisation affected employment rates in the textile industry in Bangladesh between 2010 and 2020?" is specific, focused, and researchable.

8.3 Questions That Are Too Narrow

Conversely, some questions are so narrow that they lack significance or interest. "What was the attendance rate at Tuesday morning lectures in Course XYZ during Week 5 of the autumn semester?" is a question of fact, not a research question. It can be answered by consulting a register; it does not require systematic investigation or contribute to broader understanding. The fix is to connect the narrow observation to a broader theoretical or practical concern: "How does lecture scheduling (morning vs. afternoon) relate to student attendance and academic performance in first-year courses?" (Bryman, 2016).

8.4 Questions That Are Value-Laden or Unanswerable

Questions that embed value judgements cannot be answered empirically. "Should universities abolish examinations?" is a normative question that depends on values rather than evidence. It can be reframed as an empirical question: "What do students and faculty perceive as the advantages and disadvantages of examination-based versus continuous assessment?" Similarly, questions that are purely speculative - "What would have happened if the internet had never been invented?" - cannot be investigated through data collection and should be avoided.

8.5 A Diagnostic Checklist

Figure 8.2 The diagnostic process for refining research questions.

Use this five-step diagnostic process every time you draft or revise a research question. First, write your question. Second, check its scope - is it too broad or too narrow? Third, check for bias - does it contain assumptions or value judgements? Fourth, check feasibility - can you actually investigate it with your available resources? Fifth, revise based on what you have found. Repeat until your question passes all checks.

Figure 8.2 The diagnostic process for refining research questions.

Use this five-step diagnostic process every time you draft or revise a research question. First, write your question. Second, check its scope - is it too broad or too narrow? Third, check for bias - does it contain assumptions or value judgements? Fourth, check feasibility - can you actually investigate it with your available resources? Fifth, revise based on what you have found. Repeat until your question passes all checks.

Figure 8.2 The diagnostic process for refining research questions.

Use this five-step diagnostic process every time you draft or revise a research question. First, write your question. Second, check its scope - is it too broad or too narrow? Third, check for bias - does it contain assumptions or value judgements? Fourth, check feasibility - can you actually investigate it with your available resources? Fifth, revise based on what you have found. Repeat until your question passes all checks.

To evaluate and improve your research question, apply the following diagnostic checklist: (1) Is the question clear and unambiguous? (2) Does it identify the key variables or concepts? (3) Is it specific enough to guide data collection? (4) Is it broad enough to be significant? (5) Can it be answered with available methods and data? (6) Is it free from embedded value judgements? (7) Does it address a gap in existing knowledge? If any answer is "no," revise the question until all criteria are met.

Chapter Summary

This chapter has examined the most common mistakes in research question formulation - including questions that are too broad, too narrow, value-laden, or unanswerable - and provided practical strategies for diagnosing and correcting them. A diagnostic checklist offers a systematic tool for evaluating and refining questions before committing to a study.

Key Terms

Progressive narrowing - The systematic process of refining a broad topic through successive stages of specification.

Value-laden question - A question that embeds normative judgements and cannot be answered through empirical evidence alone.

Diagnostic checklist - A structured set of criteria used to evaluate the quality and readiness of a research question.

Review Questions

1. Identify three common mistakes in research question formulation. Provide an example of each and show how it can be corrected.

2. Take a question that is too broad and demonstrate the progressive narrowing process to arrive at a focused, researchable question.

3. Why are value-laden questions problematic in research? Rewrite a value-laden question as an empirical question.

4. Apply the diagnostic checklist to a research question of your choice. Which criteria does it satisfy, and which need improvement?

8.3 Questions That Are Too Narrow

While too-broad questions are the more common problem, questions can also be too narrow. A question that is too narrow investigates a relationship so specific that the findings have limited significance. "Do left-handed third-year biology students at a particular university prefer blue pens to black pens?" is technically researchable, but the answer would contribute nothing meaningful to any body of knowledge.

The fix for a too-narrow question is to identify the broader phenomenon or principle that your specific case illustrates. What general question about human behaviour, cognition, learning, or social interaction does your specific case address? By connecting your narrow investigation to a broader scholarly conversation, you can maintain focus while ensuring that your findings have implications beyond the immediate study.

Finding the right balance between breadth and specificity is one of the central challenges of research question formulation. Your question needs to be specific enough to be investigated by a single study within a reasonable time frame, but broad enough that the answer matters to someone beyond yourself and your supervisor. The sweet spot lies in questions that address specific manifestations of general principles - questions like "Does collaborative problem-solving improve mathematical reasoning among primary school students?" which is specific enough to investigate but broad enough to inform both theory and practice.

8.4 Value-Laden and Leading Questions

A value-laden question contains embedded assumptions or judgements that bias the investigation before it has even begun. "Why is traditional classroom instruction more effective than online learning?" assumes that traditional instruction is more effective, which is an empirical question that needs to be tested, not assumed. "How has social media damaged youth mental health?" assumes that social media has damaged mental health, again presupposing the answer.

Value-laden questions are problematic because they constrain the range of possible findings. If you begin with the assumption that social media damages mental health, you are likely to design a study, select measures, and interpret findings in ways that confirm that assumption. You might focus on negative indicators (anxiety, depression, loneliness) while ignoring positive ones (social support, identity exploration, community building). You might select a sample that is more likely to show negative effects. And you might interpret ambiguous findings as supporting your assumption rather than challenging it.

The solution is to frame your question neutrally. Instead of "Why is X better than Y?" ask "How does X compare to Y?" Instead of "How has Z damaged W?" ask "What is the relationship between Z and W?" Neutral framing allows your data to speak for itself, rather than fitting it into a predetermined narrative.

Leading questions are a related but distinct problem. A leading question is one that suggests its own answer through the way it is framed. "Do students agree that technology enhances their learning?" is leading because it prompts agreement. "What are students' perceptions of the role of technology in their learning?" is neutral because it allows for any response. Leading questions are particularly problematic in survey research and interviews, where the wording of questions can significantly influence participants' responses. (Loftus & Palmer, 1974)

8.6 Learning from Published Research Questions

One of the best ways to improve your ability to formulate research questions is to study the questions used in published research. Select ten recent articles from top journals in your field and analyse their research questions. How are they structured? How specific are they? What variables do they include? How do they frame the relationship between those variables? What assumptions do they make? And how does the research question connect to the rest of the study - the methodology, the analysis, and the conclusions?

You will likely notice that published research questions share certain characteristics. They are specific but not trivial. They identify clear variables or concepts. They specify a population or context. They use neutral language. And they are framed in a way that naturally leads to the chosen methodology - descriptive questions lead to descriptive methods, causal questions lead to experimental methods, and interpretive questions lead to qualitative methods.

You might also notice that the best research questions are elegant in their simplicity. They distil complex phenomena into clear, answerable form without oversimplifying. This elegance is the result of extensive refinement - the authors almost certainly went through many iterations before arriving at the published version. Do not compare your first draft to their final product and feel discouraged; instead, study their questions as models that you can aspire to as your own question evolves through successive revisions.

8.7 The Question Revision Workshop

One of the most effective ways to improve your research question is to subject it to a structured revision process, either alone or with peers. The question revision workshop is a practical exercise that takes you through a series of checks and refinements designed to strengthen your question and prepare it for the demands of a full research project.

Begin by writing your current research question at the top of a page. Then answer the following questions about it, honestly and in writing. What are the key concepts in this question, and how will I define them? Can this question be answered through empirical investigation? Does it contain any assumptions that need to be made explicit? Is it too broad to be answered by a single study? Is it too narrow to produce findings of significance? Is it ethical? Is it feasible with my available resources? Does it make a contribution that is new and meaningful?

After answering these questions, revise your research question to address any weaknesses you have identified. Then repeat the process. Most research questions require three to five rounds of revision before they reach a form that is clear, focused, and robust enough to guide a research project. This process may feel laborious, but the investment pays enormous dividends - a well-crafted question makes every subsequent stage of your research easier and more productive.

Consider inviting peers to participate in the revision process. Give them your question and ask them to apply the same checks. Fresh eyes often spot problems that you have missed because you are too close to your own thinking. And the discussion that follows - about definitions, scope, feasibility, and significance - deepens your understanding of both your question and the broader standards of your field.

8.8 Case Studies: From Weak to Strong Questions

The following case studies illustrate the transformation of weak research questions into strong ones through the application of the principles discussed in this chapter. Each example shows the initial question, identifies its weaknesses, and demonstrates how it was refined into a more effective form.

Case Study 1: A student interested in social media began with the question: "Is social media bad for teenagers?" This question is value-laden (it assumes social media is bad), overly broad (which aspects of social media? which outcomes? which teenagers?), and binary (it expects a yes/no answer to a complex issue). Through successive refinements, the question evolved into: "What is the relationship between daily Instagram usage duration and self-reported body image satisfaction among female secondary school students aged 14-16 in urban Ghana?" This refined question identifies specific variables, a specific population, and a specific relationship to investigate.

Case Study 2: A student interested in leadership began with: "What makes a good leader?" This question is vague (what does "good" mean?), overly broad (in what context?), and difficult to operationalise for empirical research. Through refinement, it became: "How do teachers in high-performing primary schools describe the leadership practices that contribute most to their professional development and job satisfaction?" This version is specific, contextualised, and amenable to qualitative investigation.

Case Study 3: A student interested in climate change began with: "What should governments do about climate change?" This is a policy question rather than a research question - it asks for prescription rather than description or explanation. Through refinement, it became: "How do citizens in three West African coastal cities perceive the local impacts of climate change, and what factors influence their support for adaptation policies?" This version is empirically researchable and contributes to both climate science and policy studies.

These case studies illustrate that the journey from a weak question to a strong one is often dramatic. The initial questions are recognisable as genuine interests, but they lack the precision, specificity, and neutrality needed for productive research. The refined questions retain the core interest while adding the methodological rigour that makes them researchable. The transformation is not about abandoning your interests but about expressing them in a form that can be systematically investigated.

chapters.title: Chapter 9: The Anatomy of a Strong Academic Argument
chapters.content:
Chapter 9

The Anatomy of a Strong Academic Argument

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Define what constitutes an academic argument
• Identify the components of a well-structured argument: claim, evidence, warrant
• Distinguish between strong and weak academic arguments
• Construct arguments that are logically valid and evidentially supported
• Apply argument analysis techniques to published research

9.1 What Is an Academic Argument?

An academic argument is fundamentally different from an everyday argument. In everyday language, "argument" often implies conflict - two people disagreeing, perhaps emotionally. In academic discourse, an argument is simply a reasoned case for a position, supported by evidence. It is the basic building block of scholarship.

Every piece of academic writing - every journal article, thesis, dissertation, and book chapter - is, at its core, an argument. The author is making a claim and supporting it with evidence and reasoning. Understanding the structure of academic arguments allows you both to evaluate the arguments of others and to construct your own more effectively.

An academic argument is fundamentally different from an everyday argument. In everyday language, "argument" often implies conflict - two people disagreeing, perhaps emotionally. In academic discourse, an argument is simply a reasoned case for a position, supported by evidence. It is the basic building block of scholarship.

Every piece of academic writing - every journal article, thesis, dissertation, and book chapter - is, at its core, an argument. The author is making a claim and supporting it with evidence and reasoning. Understanding the structure of academic arguments allows you both to evaluate the arguments of others and to construct your own more effectively.

An academic argument is fundamentally different from an everyday argument. In everyday language, "argument" often implies conflict - two people disagreeing, perhaps emotionally. In academic discourse, an argument is simply a reasoned case for a position, supported by evidence. It is the basic building block of scholarship.

Every piece of academic writing - every journal article, thesis, dissertation, and book chapter - is, at its core, an argument. The author is making a claim and supporting it with evidence and reasoning. Understanding the structure of academic arguments allows you both to evaluate the arguments of others and to construct your own more effectively.

An academic argument is not a quarrel or a debate in the everyday sense. It is a structured, evidence-based case for a particular position, interpretation, or conclusion. Every research study, at its core, is an argument: it argues that a particular question is worth asking, that a particular method is appropriate for answering it, that the data support particular conclusions, and that those conclusions have particular implications. Understanding how to construct - and deconstruct - academic arguments is therefore fundamental to research thinking (Booth et al., 2016).

The strength of an academic argument depends not on the passion or conviction with which it is presented, but on the quality of the evidence that supports it, the soundness of the logic that connects evidence to conclusion, and the transparency with which assumptions and limitations are acknowledged. A researcher who presents a well-evidenced argument modestly will always be more persuasive to a scholarly audience than one who presents a poorly evidenced argument forcefully.

9.2 The Structure of Academic Arguments

The Structure of an Academic Argument (Toulmin, 2003)

📝

Claim

→

📊

Evidence

→

🔗

Warrant

→

🛡️

Backing

→

✅

Conclusion

Figure 9.1 The argumentation triangle. Every academic argument requires all three elements.

The claim is the position or conclusion you are arguing for. It is the answer to your research question, the point you want your reader to accept. A strong claim is specific, debatable, and significant. It should not be so obvious that no reasonable person would disagree, nor so extreme that no evidence could support it.

The evidence consists of the data, facts, observations, or findings that support your claim. In academic research, evidence must be credible (from reliable sources), relevant (directly related to the claim), sufficient (enough to make the case convincingly), and current (not outdated by more recent findings).

The warrant is the logical connection between the evidence and the claim. It explains why the evidence supports the claim. Often, warrants are implicit - the author assumes the reader will make the connection. But making warrants explicit strengthens your argument and reduces the risk of misunderstanding.

Figure 9.1 The argumentation triangle. Every academic argument requires all three elements.

The claim is the position or conclusion you are arguing for. It is the answer to your research question, the point you want your reader to accept. A strong claim is specific, debatable, and significant. It should not be so obvious that no reasonable person would disagree, nor so extreme that no evidence could support it.

The evidence consists of the data, facts, observations, or findings that support your claim. In academic research, evidence must be credible (from reliable sources), relevant (directly related to the claim), sufficient (enough to make the case convincingly), and current (not outdated by more recent findings).

The warrant is the logical connection between the evidence and the claim. It explains why the evidence supports the claim. Often, warrants are implicit - the author assumes the reader will make the connection. But making warrants explicit strengthens your argument and reduces the risk of misunderstanding.

Figure 9.1 The argumentation triangle. Every academic argument requires all three elements.

The claim is the position or conclusion you are arguing for. It is the answer to your research question, the point you want your reader to accept. A strong claim is specific, debatable, and significant. It should not be so obvious that no reasonable person would disagree, nor so extreme that no evidence could support it.

The evidence consists of the data, facts, observations, or findings that support your claim. In academic research, evidence must be credible (from reliable sources), relevant (directly related to the claim), sufficient (enough to make the case convincingly), and current (not outdated by more recent findings).

The warrant is the logical connection between the evidence and the claim. It explains why the evidence supports the claim. Often, warrants are implicit - the author assumes the reader will make the connection. But making warrants explicit strengthens your argument and reduces the risk of misunderstanding.

The philosopher Stephen Toulmin proposed a model of argumentation that remains influential in academic writing. In Toulmin's framework, an argument consists of three essential elements: the claim (the position being argued for), the evidence (the data or facts that support the claim), and the warrant (the logical principle or reasoning that connects the evidence to the claim). Additionally, arguments may include qualifications (conditions under which the claim holds), rebuttals (potential objections and responses), and backing (additional support for the warrant itself) (Toulmin, 2003).

For example, consider the following argument: "Blended learning improves student outcomes (claim) because a meta-analysis of 45 studies found significantly higher achievement scores in blended learning environments compared to purely face-to-face instruction (evidence). This is because blended learning allows students to control the pace of their learning, which enhances comprehension and retention (warrant)." Each element - claim, evidence, warrant - is necessary; without any one of them, the argument is incomplete.

9.3 Evaluating the Strength of Arguments

Criteria for Evaluating Arguments

🎯 Clarity

Is the claim clearly stated and unambiguous?

📊 Evidence Quality

Is the evidence credible, relevant, and sufficient?

🔗 Logic

Does the warrant logically connect evidence to the claim?

Not all arguments are created equal. A strong argument is one in which the claim is clearly stated, the evidence is relevant, credible, and sufficient, the warrant is logically sound, and potential objections have been anticipated and addressed. A weak argument fails on one or more of these dimensions: the claim may be vague, the evidence may be anecdotal or cherry-picked, the warrant may contain logical gaps, or objections may be ignored (Cottrell, 2017).

When evaluating arguments in published research, ask: Is the claim precisely stated? Is the evidence drawn from rigorous, peer-reviewed studies? Does the reasoning logically connect the evidence to the claim? Are alternative explanations considered? Are limitations acknowledged? These questions provide a systematic framework for distinguishing strong arguments from weak ones.

Chapter Summary

An academic argument is a structured, evidence-based case consisting of claims, evidence, and warrants. Toulmin's model provides a practical framework for constructing and evaluating arguments. Strong arguments are characterised by clear claims, credible evidence, sound reasoning, and acknowledgement of limitations and alternatives.

Key Terms

Claim - The position or conclusion that an argument seeks to establish.

Evidence - The data, facts, or findings presented in support of a claim.

Warrant - The logical principle or reasoning that connects evidence to a claim.

Toulmin model - A framework for analysing arguments consisting of claim, evidence, warrant, qualifiers, and rebuttals.

Review Questions

1. Define an academic argument and explain how it differs from an everyday argument or opinion.

2. Using Toulmin's model, analyse an argument from a published study in your field. Identify the claim, evidence, and warrant.

3. What distinguishes a strong academic argument from a weak one? Provide specific criteria.

4. Construct a brief academic argument on a topic of your choice, clearly identifying the claim, evidence, and warrant.

9.3 Evaluating the Strength of Academic Arguments

Not all academic arguments are equally strong. Learning to evaluate the strength of arguments - both your own and those of others - is a critical skill that improves with practice and experience. Strong arguments share several characteristics that distinguish them from weak or flawed ones.

First, strong arguments have clear and debatable claims. A claim that is so obvious that no reasonable person would disagree (the Earth orbits the Sun) or so vague that it is impossible to evaluate (education is important) does not constitute a useful academic claim. The best claims take a specific position on a contested issue and are articulated precisely enough that they can be tested against evidence.

Second, strong arguments are supported by sufficient, relevant, and credible evidence. Sufficient means enough evidence to make the case convincingly - a single study is rarely sufficient for a major claim. Relevant means directly related to the claim being made, not tangentially connected. And credible means from reliable sources using sound methodology.

Third, strong arguments address counterarguments. The strongest academic writing does not simply present evidence in support of a claim and ignore everything that points in the other direction. It actively seeks out and engages with opposing views, explaining why the weight of evidence favours one interpretation over another. This approach demonstrates intellectual honesty and actually strengthens your argument by showing that you have considered alternatives.

Fourth, strong arguments use appropriate hedging. Academic writing is characterised by careful qualification of claims - using phrases like "the evidence suggests," "this finding indicates," "these results are consistent with" rather than "this proves" or "this demonstrates conclusively." Hedging is not weakness; it is precision. It ensures that your claims are proportionate to your evidence, which is the hallmark of rigorous scholarship.

9.4 The Role of Theory in Academic Argumentation

Theory plays a crucial but often misunderstood role in academic argumentation. A theory is not a guess or speculation - it is a systematic framework that explains how and why certain phenomena occur. Theories organise our understanding, generate testable predictions, and provide the conceptual vocabulary that allows scholars to communicate about complex phenomena efficiently and precisely. (Abend, 2008)

In academic argumentation, theory functions as the bridge between evidence and conclusions. Raw data does not speak for itself - it must be interpreted through a theoretical lens. The same data can support different conclusions depending on the theoretical framework used to interpret it. A study finding that students who use social media more frequently report higher levels of anxiety could be interpreted through a social comparison theory lens (social media triggers upward social comparison, which causes anxiety), a displacement theory lens (social media use displaces sleep and physical activity, which causes anxiety), or a reverse causation lens (anxious individuals use social media more as a coping mechanism). (Festinger, 1954)

Understanding the theoretical landscape of your field allows you to position your argument within ongoing scholarly conversations, to draw on established concepts and frameworks, and to make your contribution legible to other scholars. It also allows you to be transparent about your assumptions - every theoretical framework carries assumptions about the nature of reality, the production of knowledge, and the importance of different factors, and making these assumptions explicit strengthens your argument by allowing readers to evaluate it on its own terms.

9.5 Constructing Extended Academic Arguments

While the basic structure of claim-evidence-warrant provides the building blocks of academic argumentation, extended academic arguments require more sophisticated architectural skills. A thesis, a dissertation, or a journal article is not a single argument but a network of interconnected arguments, each supporting the others and contributing to an overarching case.

The overall structure of an extended academic argument typically follows a logical progression. You begin by establishing the significance of your topic and identifying the gap in existing knowledge. You then present your research question and explain how answering it will contribute to filling the gap. You describe your methodology, justifying each choice and acknowledging its limitations. You present your findings, organised around themes or hypotheses. And you interpret your findings in light of existing theory and evidence, drawing conclusions that are proportionate to your data.

Within this overall structure, each section contains its own arguments. Your literature review argues that a gap exists and that your question is worth asking. Your methodology section argues that your approach is appropriate and rigorous. Your findings section argues that the data supports certain patterns or relationships. And your discussion section argues that these patterns have particular implications for theory and practice. Each of these arguments must be well-constructed in its own right, with clear claims, adequate evidence, and explicit warrants.

9.6 Argumentation in Different Genres of Academic Writing

While the fundamental structure of academic argumentation remains consistent, it manifests differently across different genres of academic writing. Understanding these genre-specific conventions helps you tailor your arguments for maximum impact in whatever context you are writing.

In a journal article, argumentation is typically tight, focused, and economical. Space is limited, and every paragraph must contribute directly to the central argument. The introduction establishes the significance of the topic and identifies the gap. The literature review builds the case for the study's contribution. The methodology section argues that the chosen approach is appropriate and rigorous. The results section presents the evidence. And the discussion section argues for a particular interpretation of the results and considers their implications. (Swales, 1990)

In a thesis or dissertation, argumentation is more expansive.
