Several structured frameworks exist for critical appraisal, each tailored to different types of research. The CASP (Critical Appraisal Skills Programme) checklists, for example, provide structured questions for evaluating randomised controlled trials, qualitative studies, systematic reviews, and other study designs. Using these frameworks ensures that your appraisal is systematic and comprehensive, rather than impressionistic and partial. (Critical Appraisal Skills Programme, 2018)

Key questions in critical appraisal include: Was the research question clearly stated? Was the methodology appropriate for the question? Were the participants appropriately selected? Were the measurements valid and reliable? Were potential confounding variables controlled for? Were the statistical analyses appropriate? Do the conclusions follow from the results? Were the limitations adequately discussed? These questions apply, with modifications, to both quantitative and qualitative research.

Developing strong critical appraisal skills takes time and practice. Start by appraising studies in areas you know well, where you can draw on your content knowledge to evaluate the study's claims. As your skills develop, apply them to studies in less familiar areas, where you must rely more heavily on methodological knowledge. And share your appraisals with peers - discussing your evaluations helps refine your judgement and exposes you to different perspectives on quality assessment.

14.6 Reading with Purpose: Different Approaches for Different Goals

Not all reading serves the same purpose, and effective readers adapt their approach depending on what they need from a text. Understanding the different purposes of reading and matching your approach to your purpose dramatically increases the efficiency and productivity of your reading time.

Scanning is the most superficial form of reading, used when you need to quickly determine whether a text is relevant to your needs. Scan the title, abstract, introduction, and conclusion to decide whether the text warrants a more careful reading. This approach is essential for managing the large volumes of literature that accumulate during a thorough literature search.

Skimming is a slightly more engaged form of reading, used when you want a general understanding of a text's main arguments and findings without reading every word. Focus on headings, topic sentences, figures, and tables. This approach is useful for getting an overview of a field or catching up on a topic that is peripheral to your main research.

Close reading is the most intensive form, used when you need to understand a text in detail and evaluate it critically. Read every word, annotate as you go, and pause regularly to reflect on what you have read. This approach should be reserved for the most important and relevant texts in your collection, because it is time-consuming but also the most productive form of reading for deep learning.

14.7 From Reading to Writing: Bridging the Gap

Many students struggle with the transition from reading to writing - they read extensively but find it difficult to transform their reading into written arguments. This difficulty is common and can be addressed through specific strategies that bridge the gap between consuming knowledge and producing it.

The first strategy is to write while you read. Do not wait until you have finished reading to begin writing. Instead, write notes, summaries, and reflections as you go. After each article or chapter, write a brief response: What did you learn? How does it connect to what you already know? What questions does it raise? Where do you agree or disagree? These writing-while-reading exercises serve a dual purpose: they help you process and retain what you have read, and they generate raw material that you can later incorporate into your formal writing.

The second strategy is to use reading to develop your argument, not just your knowledge. As you read, ask yourself not just "What does this source say?" but "How does this source relate to my argument?" Each source you read should change your argument in some way - strengthening a point, introducing a nuance, raising a counter-argument, or suggesting a new direction. If a source does not affect your argument, it may not belong in your literature review.

The third strategy is to read with structure in mind. As you read multiple sources on the same topic, look for the structural patterns that connect them. Do they address the same themes? Do they use similar methods? Do they reach similar conclusions? These structural patterns provide the organisational framework for your literature review. When you sit down to write, you are not starting from scratch - you are arranging sources within a structure that your reading has already revealed.

The fourth strategy is to practice free writing. Set a timer for ten minutes and write continuously about your research topic without stopping to edit, revise, or worry about quality. This exercise helps overcome the paralysis that often accompanies the transition from reading to writing, and it often produces surprisingly useful material. Much of what you write in a free writing session will need to be revised or discarded, but the ideas that emerge can provide starting points for more structured writing. (Elbow, 1998)

chapters.title: Chapter 15: Positioning Your Research Within an Existing Field
chapters.content:
Chapter 15

Positioning Your Research Within an Existing Field

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Explain what it means to position research within an existing field
• Map the intellectual landscape of a research area
• Identify your study's relationship to existing theories, debates, and findings
• Write a compelling positioning statement for your research
• Communicate the unique contribution of your study clearly

15.1 The Importance of Positioning

Positioning your research is like placing a new building in an existing cityscape. You need to show how your building relates to the structures already there - how it fills a gap, extends a street, or provides a view that was previously unavailable. Without positioning, your research floats in isolation, disconnected from the conversations that give it meaning.

Positioning your research is like placing a new building in an existing cityscape. You need to show how your building relates to the structures already there - how it fills a gap, extends a street, or provides a view that was previously unavailable. Without positioning, your research floats in isolation, disconnected from the conversations that give it meaning.

Positioning your research is like placing a new building in an existing cityscape. You need to show how your building relates to the structures already there - how it fills a gap, extends a street, or provides a view that was previously unavailable. Without positioning, your research floats in isolation, disconnected from the conversations that give it meaning.

Every piece of research exists in relation to other research. No study stands alone - it builds on what has come before, addresses gaps that others have identified, extends or challenges existing theories, and contributes to an ongoing scholarly conversation. Positioning your research means making these relationships explicit: showing where your study fits within the broader landscape of knowledge and explaining how it relates to the work of other scholars (Booth et al., 2016).

Effective positioning serves multiple purposes. It demonstrates that you have a thorough understanding of the field - that you know what has been done, what debates are ongoing, and where the frontiers of knowledge lie. It establishes the credibility of your study by showing that it is grounded in existing knowledge. And it highlights the unique contribution of your work by making clear what your study adds that previous studies have not provided.

15.2 Mapping the Intellectual Landscape

Positioning Your Study in the Field

📚

What is known

→

❓

What is debated

→

🕳️

What is missing

→

🎯

Your contribution

Figure 15.1 The four-step process for positioning your study within existing scholarship.

Mapping the intellectual landscape involves identifying the major schools of thought, key debates, landmark studies, and evolving trends in your field. This is not a summary of everything that has been written - it is a strategic overview that shows how your research question fits into the bigger picture.

Figure 15.1 The four-step process for positioning your study within existing scholarship.

Mapping the intellectual landscape involves identifying the major schools of thought, key debates, landmark studies, and evolving trends in your field. This is not a summary of everything that has been written - it is a strategic overview that shows how your research question fits into the bigger picture.

Figure 15.1 The four-step process for positioning your study within existing scholarship.

Mapping the intellectual landscape involves identifying the major schools of thought, key debates, landmark studies, and evolving trends in your field. This is not a summary of everything that has been written - it is a strategic overview that shows how your research question fits into the bigger picture.

Before you can position your research, you must map the intellectual landscape of your field. This involves identifying the key theories, landmark studies, ongoing debates, dominant methods, and emerging trends in your area. Tools such as citation analysis, bibliometric mapping, and literature review matrices can help you visualise the structure of the field and identify clusters of related work, influential authors, and underexplored areas.

A useful exercise is to create a positioning matrix - a table that lists the major studies in your area along with their key variables, methods, findings, and limitations. This matrix allows you to see at a glance what has been done and where your study fills a gap.

15.3 Writing Your Positioning Statement

A positioning statement is a concise paragraph that articulates how your study relates to existing research. It typically follows this structure: "Previous research has established X (citation). However, Y remains understudied/unresolved/contradictory (citation). This study addresses this gap by investigating Z using [method] with [population/context]. The findings will contribute to [theory/practice/policy] by [specific contribution]." This statement, usually placed at the end of a literature review or introduction, provides the logical bridge between what is known and what your study will add.

Chapter Summary

Positioning research within an existing field involves mapping the intellectual landscape, identifying relationships to existing work, and articulating a clear contribution. A positioning matrix and a well-crafted positioning statement communicate the significance and originality of a study to readers and reviewers.

Key Terms

Research positioning - The process of explicitly situating a study within the broader landscape of existing knowledge and scholarship.

Positioning matrix - A tabular tool listing key studies with their variables, methods, findings, and limitations for comparative analysis.

Positioning statement - A concise articulation of how a study relates to existing research and what unique contribution it makes.

Review Questions

1. Why is it important to position your research within an existing field? What are the consequences of failing to do so?

2. Create a positioning matrix for a topic in your field. What patterns and gaps does it reveal?

3. Write a positioning statement for a research question of your choice.

15.3 Writing a Compelling Literature Review

The literature review is the section of your thesis or dissertation where positioning becomes visible. It is not a summary of everything you have read - it is a strategic argument that establishes the context for your research question, identifies the gap you intend to fill, and demonstrates your mastery of the relevant scholarship.

A common mistake in literature reviews is to organise them chronologically or by author - reviewing one study, then another, then another, in sequence. This approach produces a descriptive list rather than an analytical argument. A more effective approach is to organise your review thematically, grouping studies by the aspects of your topic they address and weaving your own analysis through the discussion of each theme. (Ridley, 2012)

Within each thematic section, aim for synthesis rather than summary. Instead of describing what each study found, compare and contrast findings across studies. Identify areas of agreement and disagreement. Explain why different studies might have reached different conclusions. And use these observations to build toward your own argument about what the literature tells us and what it does not.

The literature review should culminate in a clear statement of the gap that your study addresses. This gap should flow naturally from the preceding analysis - after reading your literature review, the reader should feel that your research question is not only logical but necessary. The gap should be framed not as a mere absence of research but as a significant limitation in our current understanding that has consequences for theory, practice, or policy.

15.4 Positioning Across Different Scholarly Traditions

Positioning your research effectively requires awareness of the scholarly tradition in which you are working. Different traditions have different expectations for how research is positioned, and failing to meet these expectations can result in work that is poorly received, not because of its quality but because of its framing.

In quantitative traditions, positioning typically involves identifying a gap in the empirical evidence, reviewing the relevant theories and prior findings, and formulating hypotheses that your study will test. The emphasis is on building on and extending previous work in a cumulative fashion, with each study adding a brick to the wall of knowledge.

In qualitative traditions, positioning may involve identifying an aspect of human experience that has not been adequately explored or understood. The emphasis is less on testing hypotheses and more on generating new insights, developing conceptual frameworks, or giving voice to perspectives that have been marginalised in previous research. Qualitative positioning often involves a more reflexive engagement with the researcher's own assumptions and perspective.

In critical traditions, positioning involves identifying structures of power, inequality, or injustice that previous research has failed to address or has inadvertently reinforced. The emphasis is on challenging taken-for-granted assumptions, revealing hidden dynamics, and contributing to social change. Critical positioning requires not just scholarly competence but political awareness and ethical commitment.

15.5 Common Positioning Mistakes

Several common mistakes can undermine the effectiveness of your positioning. Being aware of these mistakes helps you avoid them in your own work and recognise them in the work of others.

The most common mistake is positioning against a straw man - misrepresenting previous research in order to make your own contribution appear more significant. For example, claiming that "previous research has completely ignored the role of context" when in fact several studies have addressed context, but in ways that differ from your approach. This kind of exaggeration may seem like an effective strategy, but it damages your credibility with readers who are familiar with the literature you are misrepresenting. (Sandberg & Alvesson, 2011)

A second common mistake is over-claiming your contribution. Every study makes a contribution, but not every contribution is revolutionary. Positioning your masters thesis as a paradigm shift or your pilot study as definitive evidence will invite scepticism. Instead, position your contribution accurately and proportionately: "This study extends our understanding of X by examining it in the previously unstudied context of Y." (Hyland, 2005)

A third mistake is failing to position at all. Some researchers, particularly those early in their careers, present their work without explaining how it relates to existing scholarship. This makes it difficult for readers to evaluate the study's contribution and significance. Even if your study replicates a well-established finding in a new context, you need to explain why this replication matters and what it adds to the field.

A fourth mistake is positioning your work only within your own narrow subfield. While it is essential to connect your work to the most directly relevant literature, also consider positioning it within broader scholarly conversations. A study of mobile learning in Ghana, for example, might be positioned not only within the mobile learning literature but also within broader conversations about educational technology, digital inequality, and the globalisation of higher education. This broader positioning increases the potential impact and readership of your work.

15.6 Positioning in Practice: A Step-by-Step Guide

Positioning your research effectively requires systematic effort. The following step-by-step guide will help you develop a strong position for your study.

Step 1: Identify the major conversations in your field. Read recent review articles, handbook chapters, and editorial commentaries to understand the key debates, trends, and concerns that currently animate your field. Make a list of the three to five most important conversations that relate to your research topic.

Step 2: Map the positions in each conversation. For each conversation, identify the major positions that scholars hold. Who are the key proponents of each position? What evidence do they cite? Where do they agree and disagree? This mapping exercise gives you a detailed picture of the intellectual landscape in which your study will be situated.

Step 3: Identify your entry point. Based on your mapping, determine where your study fits. Are you supporting one position with new evidence? Challenging an established position? Proposing a synthesis of competing positions? Introducing a new perspective that has not been considered? Your entry point should be specific, defensible, and clearly connected to the existing conversation.

Step 4: Articulate your contribution. Write a clear, concise statement of what your study adds to the conversation. This statement should explain what is new about your study, why it matters, and how it changes or enriches the existing understanding. This contribution statement will serve as the organising principle for your literature review and will appear prominently in your introduction and discussion sections.

Step 5: Test your positioning with others. Share your positioning statement with your supervisor, peers, and other scholars in your field. Ask whether they find it clear, convincing, and significant. Their feedback will help you refine your positioning and ensure that it resonates with your intended audience.

chapters.title: Chapter 16: Building an Original Intellectual Contribution
chapters.content:
Chapter 16

Building an Original Intellectual Contribution

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Define what constitutes an original intellectual contribution in research
• Identify different types of originality in academic work
• Assess the originality of your own research
• Communicate your contribution clearly and persuasively
• Understand how originality is evaluated by examiners and reviewers

16.1 What Makes Research Original?

Originality in research does not mean that no one has ever thought about your topic. It means that your specific combination of question, method, context, and analysis produces knowledge that did not previously exist. This is a much more achievable standard than most students realise, and understanding the different types of originality can help you identify where your contribution lies.

Originality in research does not mean that no one has ever thought about your topic. It means that your specific combination of question, method, context, and analysis produces knowledge that did not previously exist. This is a much more achievable standard than most students realise, and understanding the different types of originality can help you identify where your contribution lies.

Originality in research does not mean that no one has ever thought about your topic. It means that your specific combination of question, method, context, and analysis produces knowledge that did not previously exist. This is a much more achievable standard than most students realise, and understanding the different types of originality can help you identify where your contribution lies.

Originality is one of the most frequently cited criteria for evaluating research quality, yet it is also one of the most poorly understood. Many students believe that originality requires a dramatic breakthrough - a paradigm-shifting discovery that overturns established knowledge. In reality, most original research makes incremental contributions that advance understanding in a particular area without revolutionising the entire field. Originality can take many forms, and understanding these forms can help you identify and articulate the unique contribution of your own work (Phillips & Pugh, 2015).

16.2 Types of Originality

Types of Original Contribution

🆕 New Evidence

Providing empirical evidence where none existed before.

🔬 New Method

Applying a method not previously used to study this phenomenon.

🧠 New Theory

Proposing a new theoretical framework or modifying an existing one.

🌍 New Context

Studying a known phenomenon in a new population, setting, or culture.

🔗 New Synthesis

Combining insights from multiple studies into a new understanding.

🔄 Replication

Confirming or challenging existing findings with rigorous methodology.

Figure 16.1 Four types of original contribution, classified by approach and level.

Figure 16.1 Four types of original contribution, classified by approach and level.

Figure 16.1 Four types of original contribution, classified by approach and level.

Originality in research can manifest in several ways. You might ask a new question that has not been previously investigated. You might study an existing question in a new context - applying theories developed in one culture, country, or population to a different setting. You might use a new methodology to investigate a well-studied phenomenon, potentially revealing aspects that previous methods missed. You might propose a new theoretical framework that provides a better explanation for existing findings. You might synthesise previously disconnected bodies of literature, revealing connections that were not previously apparent. Or you might replicate a study in a new setting, testing the generalisability of previous findings (Bryman, 2016).

Each of these forms of originality represents a genuine contribution to knowledge. The key is to be clear about which type of originality your study offers and to communicate this clearly in your writing.

16.3 Communicating Your Contribution

The ability to articulate your contribution clearly and persuasively is essential for success in research. Examiners, reviewers, and funders need to understand not just what you did, but why it matters and what it adds to existing knowledge. Your contribution statement should be specific: rather than claiming vaguely that your study "fills a gap," explain precisely what the gap is, how your study addresses it, and what the field gains as a result. The most effective contribution statements connect the specific findings to broader theoretical, practical, or policy implications (Creswell & Creswell, 2018).

Chapter Summary

Originality in research takes many forms, from new questions and contexts to new methods and theoretical frameworks. Understanding and articulating the specific type of originality your study offers is essential for communicating its value to the scholarly community. A clear contribution statement connects your findings to broader theoretical and practical significance.

Key Terms

Original contribution - A new addition to existing knowledge achieved through novel questions, methods, contexts, or theoretical perspectives.

Incremental contribution - A modest but meaningful advance in understanding that extends or refines existing knowledge.

Contribution statement - A clear articulation of what a study adds to existing knowledge and why this addition matters.

Review Questions

1. Identify at least five different types of originality in research. Which type is most relevant to your current or planned work?

2. Why is it insufficient to simply claim that your study "fills a gap"? What additional information is needed?

3. Write a contribution statement for a research project, specifying the type of originality and the broader significance.

16.3 Demonstrating Originality in Your Research

One of the most common anxieties among graduate students is the fear that their research is not sufficiently original. This anxiety is usually unfounded, but it can be paralysing if left unaddressed. Understanding what originality means in practice - and recognising that there are many different ways to be original - can help alleviate this anxiety and give you the confidence to pursue your research with conviction.

Originality in research is not about making a revolutionary breakthrough that overturns everything we thought we knew. Such breakthroughs are extraordinarily rare, and expecting one from a masters or doctoral thesis is unrealistic. Rather, originality means contributing something new - however modest - to the conversation in your field. This contribution might be new data from a context that has not been studied. It might be a new application of an existing method. It might be a new synthesis of existing findings that reveals a pattern no one had previously noticed. Or it might be a new interpretation of familiar data that challenges conventional understanding. (Phillips & Pugh, 2010)

To demonstrate originality effectively, you need to be explicit about what is new in your work and why it matters. This is best done by clearly articulating the gap in existing knowledge that your study addresses and explaining precisely how your study fills that gap. Do not leave it to the reader to figure out what is original about your work - tell them directly, clearly, and persuasively.

It is also helpful to position your originality relative to a specific scholarly conversation. Rather than claiming to be original in the abstract, show that you are making a specific contribution to a specific debate. "This study provides the first quantitative evidence on the relationship between mobile learning app usage and academic performance among Ghanaian university students" is a clear and specific claim of originality that positions your work within a particular conversation and makes your contribution easy to evaluate.

16.4 The Courage to Be Original

Originality requires courage. By definition, original research takes you into territory that has not been explored before. There are no maps, no established procedures, and no guarantee that you will find anything interesting. This uncertainty can be deeply unsettling, especially for students who have spent their previous academic careers following clear instructions and seeking right answers.

The courage to be original also involves the willingness to be wrong. When you propose a new idea, a new interpretation, or a new framework, you are putting yourself out there in a way that invites criticism. Your idea might be rejected by reviewers. Your interpretation might be challenged by more experienced scholars. Your framework might prove inadequate. These are real risks, and they can feel personally threatening. But they are also the conditions under which genuine intellectual growth occurs.

Developing this courage requires a shift in mindset. Instead of viewing criticism as a personal attack, learn to see it as a gift - an opportunity to strengthen your thinking and improve your work. Instead of viewing failure as a reflection of your worth, learn to see it as a natural and necessary part of the research process. And instead of waiting until you feel confident before acting, learn to act despite your uncertainty, trusting that confidence will develop through doing rather than through waiting.

16.5 Originality and the Fear of the Literature

Many beginning researchers develop what might be called a fear of the literature - a worry that reading more will make it harder to be original, because they will discover that their ideas have already been explored. This fear is understandable but misguided. In reality, the opposite is true: the more you read, the more original you can be, because you develop a clearer picture of what has already been done and where genuine opportunities for originality lie.

The fear of the literature often leads to avoidance - students read less than they should, hoping to preserve the freshness of their ideas. But ideas developed in ignorance of the literature are not original; they are uninformed. They may duplicate what has already been done, miss important nuances that previous research has revealed, or pursue approaches that have already been shown to be unproductive. By contrast, ideas developed through deep engagement with the literature are both more original and more valuable, because they are positioned at the frontier of knowledge rather than behind it.

The key insight is that originality in research is not about having ideas that no one has ever had. It is about knowing the landscape of existing ideas well enough to identify where something new can be built. This requires extensive reading, not avoidance of it. The researcher who has read five hundred papers on their topic is far better positioned to make an original contribution than the one who has read five, because the former knows where the field has been, where it is heading, and where the unexplored territories lie.

Think of the literature not as a constraint on your originality but as a launching pad for it. Every study you read is a potential springboard for your own work - a source of questions, methods, findings, and ideas that you can build on, challenge, or extend. The richest soil for original ideas lies at the intersection of what is known and what is not known, and you can only find that intersection through thorough engagement with the existing scholarship.

16.6 Originality and Replication

In recent years, the research community has engaged in a robust debate about the relationship between originality and replication. The traditional emphasis on originality - on producing new findings, testing new hypotheses, and proposing new theories - has sometimes been seen as being in tension with the equally important scientific practice of replication - repeating previous studies to verify their findings.

The replication crisis - the discovery that many published findings in psychology, medicine, economics, and other fields cannot be reproduced when the studies are repeated - has highlighted the importance of replication as a corrective to the bias toward novelty. If researchers are rewarded primarily for producing original findings, they may have insufficient incentive to verify the findings of others. And if journals are reluctant to publish replication studies because they lack novelty, the self-correcting mechanism of science is weakened. (Open Science Collaboration, 2015)

However, replication and originality are not inherently opposed. A well-designed replication study can be highly original if it tests an established finding in a new context, with a new population, or using improved methods. "Conceptual replications" - studies that test the same underlying hypothesis using different operational definitions and methods - are particularly valuable because they assess not just whether a finding is reliable but whether it is robust across different approaches. (Schmidt, 2009)

For emerging researchers, replication studies offer an excellent entry point into a research field. They provide a clear and well-defined research question, an established methodology that can be adapted, and a built-in framework for interpreting results. If your replication confirms the original finding, you have contributed to the reliability of the evidence base. If it fails to confirm the original finding, you have identified an important discrepancy that calls for further investigation. Either way, you have made a genuine contribution to knowledge.

The key is to approach replication not as a mechanical repetition of someone else's work but as a thoughtful and rigorous investigation that adds value to the evidence base. A good replication study explains clearly why replication is needed (perhaps the original study had methodological limitations, or the finding has not been tested in your context), describes any modifications to the original design and the reasons for them, and discusses the implications of the results for the reliability and generalisability of the original findings.

chapters.title: Chapter 17: Recognising Your Own Assumptions Before You Research
chapters.content:
Chapter 17

Recognising Your Own Assumptions Before You Research

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Define assumptions and explain their role in shaping research
• Identify your own personal, cultural, and disciplinary assumptions
• Understand how unexamined assumptions can bias research design and interpretation
• Apply techniques for surfacing and examining hidden assumptions
• Develop an assumption audit for your own research projects

17.1 What Are Assumptions?

Assumptions are beliefs we hold so deeply that we rarely examine them. They are the invisible lenses through which we view the world, shaping what we see, what we ask, and what we consider valid evidence. In everyday life, assumptions are useful shortcuts. In research, they can be dangerous distortions.

Assumptions are beliefs we hold so deeply that we rarely examine them. They are the invisible lenses through which we view the world, shaping what we see, what we ask, and what we consider valid evidence. In everyday life, assumptions are useful shortcuts. In research, they can be dangerous distortions.

Assumptions are beliefs we hold so deeply that we rarely examine them. They are the invisible lenses through which we view the world, shaping what we see, what we ask, and what we consider valid evidence. In everyday life, assumptions are useful shortcuts. In research, they can be dangerous distortions.

Every researcher brings assumptions to their work - beliefs, values, and taken-for-granted ideas that shape how they see the world, what questions they ask, and how they interpret their findings. Assumptions are not inherently problematic; they are unavoidable. The danger lies in assumptions that remain unexamined, because unrecognised assumptions can silently distort every stage of the research process, from question formulation to data analysis to the interpretation of results (Creswell & Creswell, 2018).

Assumptions operate at multiple levels. Personal assumptions derive from your individual experiences, beliefs, and worldview. Cultural assumptions reflect the norms, values, and practices of the society or community in which you were raised. Disciplinary assumptions are embedded in the theories, methods, and conventions of your academic field. Each type of assumption can influence your research in ways that are difficult to detect without deliberate reflection.

17.2 How Assumptions Shape Research

Figure 17.1 The five layers of assumptions that shape every research project.

Each layer of assumption influences the layers below it. Your ontological assumptions (what you believe about the nature of reality) shape your epistemological assumptions (what you believe about how knowledge is produced), which in turn shape your methodological choices. Making these layers explicit is essential for intellectual honesty and methodological coherence.

Figure 17.1 The five layers of assumptions that shape every research project.

Each layer of assumption influences the layers below it. Your ontological assumptions (what you believe about the nature of reality) shape your epistemological assumptions (what you believe about how knowledge is produced), which in turn shape your methodological choices. Making these layers explicit is essential for intellectual honesty and methodological coherence.

Figure 17.1 The five layers of assumptions that shape every research project.

Each layer of assumption influences the layers below it. Your ontological assumptions (what you believe about the nature of reality) shape your epistemological assumptions (what you believe about how knowledge is produced), which in turn shape your methodological choices. Making these layers explicit is essential for intellectual honesty and methodological coherence.

Consider a researcher who assumes that economic incentives are the primary driver of human behaviour. This assumption - rooted in rational choice theory - will lead the researcher to design studies that focus on financial variables, to interpret data through an economic lens, and to overlook social, cultural, emotional, and relational factors that may be equally or more important. The assumption does not make the research wrong, but it makes it partial - it produces a particular view of reality while obscuring others.

Similarly, a researcher who assumes that quantitative data are inherently more rigorous than qualitative data may design studies that privilege measurement over meaning, potentially missing the rich, contextual insights that qualitative methods can provide. These methodological assumptions are rarely stated explicitly but profoundly shape the kind of knowledge that research produces (Bryman, 2016).

17.3 Surfacing Your Assumptions: An Assumption Audit

The Assumption Audit: Key Questions
CategoryAudit Question
OntologicalWhat do I believe about the nature of reality? Is it fixed or socially constructed?
EpistemologicalWhat do I believe about how knowledge is produced? Through measurement or interpretation?
PersonalWhat life experiences shape how I see this topic?
TheoreticalWhich theories do I favour — and why? What am I taking for granted?
MethodologicalWhy have I chosen this method? What assumptions does it carry?
The Assumption Audit: Key Questions
CategoryAudit Question
OntologicalWhat do I believe about the nature of reality? Is it fixed or socially constructed?
EpistemologicalWhat do I believe about how knowledge is produced? Through measurement or interpretation?
PersonalWhat life experiences shape how I see this topic?
TheoreticalWhich theories do I favour — and why? What am I taking for granted?
MethodologicalWhy have I chosen this method? What assumptions does it carry?
The Assumption Audit: Key Questions
CategoryAudit Question
OntologicalWhat do I believe about the nature of reality? Is it fixed or socially constructed?
EpistemologicalWhat do I believe about how knowledge is produced? Through measurement or interpretation?
PersonalWhat life experiences shape how I see this topic?
TheoreticalWhich theories do I favour - and why? What am I taking for granted?
MethodologicalWhy have I chosen this method? What assumptions does it carry?

An assumption audit is a structured exercise in which you systematically identify and examine the assumptions underlying your research. Begin by asking yourself: What do I already believe about this topic? What do I expect to find? Why do I expect this? What theoretical perspective am I bringing? What cultural or personal experiences shape my view? What methodological preferences do I have, and why? Write your answers down and discuss them with a trusted colleague or supervisor. The goal is not to eliminate assumptions - which is impossible - but to make them visible so that they can be critically examined and their potential effects on the research can be anticipated and managed (Kumar, 2019). (Blaikie, 2010)

Chapter Summary

Every researcher carries personal, cultural, and disciplinary assumptions that shape their work. Unexamined assumptions can bias question formulation, research design, and interpretation. An assumption audit is a practical tool for surfacing hidden assumptions and managing their influence on the research process.

Key Terms

Assumption - A belief or idea taken for granted that shapes perception, reasoning, and research decisions.

Assumption audit - A structured exercise for identifying and examining the assumptions underlying a research project.

Disciplinary assumption - Taken-for-granted beliefs embedded in the theories, methods, and conventions of an academic field.

Review Questions

1. Why are unexamined assumptions dangerous in research? Provide a specific example.

2. Identify three of your own assumptions about a topic you are interested in researching. How might these assumptions influence your study design?

3. Conduct an assumption audit for a research question of your choice. What did you discover?

17.3 The Assumption Audit

An assumption audit is a systematic process of identifying and examining the assumptions that underlie your research. It involves stepping back from your project and asking difficult questions about what you are taking for granted. This process is uncomfortable but essential, because unexamined assumptions can undermine the validity of even the most carefully designed study.

Begin your audit at the ontological level. What are you assuming about the nature of the phenomenon you are studying? If you are studying "student motivation," are you assuming that motivation is a stable individual trait, a dynamic state that fluctuates with circumstances, or a social construction that exists in the interaction between individuals and their environments? Your answer to this question has profound implications for your research design, your choice of instruments, and your interpretation of findings.

Move next to the epistemological level. What are you assuming about how knowledge of this phenomenon can be produced? Are you assuming that motivation can be measured objectively through self-report questionnaires, or that it must be understood through in-depth qualitative exploration? Are you assuming that your findings will be generalisable to other populations and contexts, or that they will be specific to the particular context you studied?

Then examine your methodological assumptions. Why have you chosen this particular method? What does it allow you to see, and what does it obscure? Every method is both a lens and a filter - it brings some aspects of reality into sharp focus while blurring or hiding others. Being explicit about what your method can and cannot reveal is essential for honest scholarship.

Finally, examine your personal assumptions. What life experiences, cultural background, professional training, and personal beliefs might influence how you approach your research? These personal assumptions are often the most difficult to identify because they feel natural and obvious rather than contestable. But they are no less influential for being invisible, and surfacing them is essential for reflexive, self-aware scholarship.

17.4 Living with Your Assumptions

The goal of examining your assumptions is not to eliminate them - that is impossible. Every research project rests on assumptions, and trying to conduct research without any assumptions would paralyse you before you began. The goal is to make your assumptions explicit, to evaluate whether they are justified, and to be transparent about them in your research report.

Making your assumptions explicit has several benefits. It allows readers to evaluate your work more fairly, because they can see the foundations on which your argument is built. It demonstrates intellectual maturity and reflexive awareness, which are highly valued in academic work. And it protects you from the criticism that you have been naive or unthinking about the foundations of your research.

Being transparent about your assumptions also opens up productive scholarly conversations. When two researchers reach different conclusions about the same phenomenon, the difference can often be traced to different underlying assumptions. By making these assumptions explicit, you make it possible to have a productive discussion about which assumptions are more justified, rather than a fruitless argument about which conclusions are correct.

17.5 Assumptions and Paradigm Choice

Your assumptions about the nature of reality and the production of knowledge are intimately connected to your choice of research paradigm. A paradigm is a comprehensive worldview that includes ontological assumptions (what is real), epistemological assumptions (how we can know about reality), and methodological assumptions (how we should investigate reality). Understanding the relationship between assumptions and paradigms is essential for designing coherent research and for communicating effectively with scholars who work within different paradigms.

The positivist paradigm assumes that reality exists independently of human perception, that it can be measured objectively, and that the goal of research is to discover general laws that explain and predict phenomena. Researchers working within this paradigm typically use quantitative methods, seek large representative samples, and evaluate their work using criteria such as validity, reliability, and generalisability. (Comte, 1853/2009)

The interpretivist paradigm assumes that reality is socially constructed, that multiple valid interpretations of any phenomenon exist, and that the goal of research is to understand the meanings that people attach to their experiences. Researchers working within this paradigm typically use qualitative methods, study smaller groups in depth, and evaluate their work using criteria such as credibility, transferability, and authenticity. (Schwandt, 2000)

The critical paradigm assumes that reality is shaped by structures of power and inequality, that knowledge production is never politically neutral, and that the goal of research is not just to understand the world but to change it. Researchers working within this paradigm often combine qualitative and quantitative methods, focus on issues of justice and equity, and evaluate their work in terms of its contribution to social transformation. (Kincheloe & McLaren, 2011)

Choosing a paradigm is not a purely intellectual exercise - it reflects your deepest beliefs about the nature of reality and the purpose of research. But it is important to be explicit about your paradigmatic commitments, because they shape every aspect of your research, from the questions you ask to the methods you use to the conclusions you draw. Making these commitments visible allows others to evaluate your work on its own terms, rather than applying the criteria of a different paradigm and finding it wanting.

17.6 Assumptions in Collaborative Research

When you work with other researchers - as co-investigators, supervisors, or members of a research team - the challenge of managing assumptions becomes more complex. Different team members may bring different assumptions to the project, and these differences, if unexamined, can lead to confusion, conflict, and inconsistency in the research.

The most effective research teams address assumptions explicitly at the beginning of a project. They discuss their ontological, epistemological, and methodological assumptions openly, identify areas of agreement and disagreement, and negotiate a shared framework that all team members can work within. This negotiation process can be time-consuming, but it prevents much larger problems down the line.

In interdisciplinary research teams, assumption differences are particularly pronounced. A psychologist and an economist studying the same phenomenon may hold fundamentally different assumptions about human behaviour, the role of context, and the appropriate methods for investigation. Making these differences explicit and finding ways to bridge them is essential for producing coherent interdisciplinary research.

Supervisory relationships also involve assumption management. Your supervisor may hold assumptions that differ from yours, and these differences can create tension if they are not addressed. For example, if your supervisor assumes a positivist framework while you are drawn to interpretivism, this difference needs to be discussed early and resolved. Sometimes the resolution involves adjusting your approach to align with your supervisor's expertise; sometimes it involves negotiating a compromise; and sometimes it involves finding a supervisor whose assumptions are more compatible with yours.

chapters.title: Chapter 18: Types of Bias That Distort Research Thinking
chapters.content:
Chapter 18

Types of Bias That Distort Research Thinking

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Define bias in the context of research and distinguish it from error
• Identify the major types of cognitive, methodological, and social bias
• Explain how each type of bias can distort research design, data collection, and analysis
• Apply strategies for detecting and mitigating bias in your own research
• Evaluate published research for potential sources of bias

18.1 Understanding Bias in Research

Bias in research is any systematic tendency that distorts findings away from the truth. It can enter the research process at any stage - from question formulation to data collection to interpretation of results - and it is often invisible to the researcher themselves. Understanding the major types of bias is the first step toward guarding against them.

Bias in research is any systematic tendency that distorts findings away from the truth. It can enter the research process at any stage - from question formulation to data collection to interpretation of results - and it is often invisible to the researcher themselves. Understanding the major types of bias is the first step toward guarding against them.

Bias in research is any systematic tendency that distorts findings away from the truth. It can enter the research process at any stage - from question formulation to data collection to interpretation of results - and it is often invisible to the researcher themselves. Understanding the major types of bias is the first step toward guarding against them.

Bias in research refers to any systematic tendency that produces results that differ from the truth. Unlike random error, which affects results unpredictably and can be reduced by increasing sample size, bias is systematic - it consistently pushes results in a particular direction, and increasing sample size does not correct it. Bias can enter the research process at any stage: in the formulation of questions, the design of studies, the selection of participants, the collection of data, the analysis of results, and the interpretation of findings (Bryman, 2016).

18.2 Cognitive Biases

Key Cognitive Biases in Research

🔍 Confirmation Bias

Seeking evidence that confirms your existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence. (Nickerson, 1998)

📰 Availability Bias

Overestimating the likelihood of events that are easily recalled or vivid.

⚓ Anchoring Bias

Relying too heavily on the first piece of information encountered.

📊 Hindsight Bias

Believing, after learning an outcome, that you would have predicted it.

Figure 18.1 Six cognitive biases that commonly affect research thinking.

Confirmation bias is the most pervasive and dangerous of all cognitive biases. It is the tendency to seek out, notice, and remember information that confirms your existing beliefs while ignoring or dismissing information that contradicts them. Every researcher is susceptible to confirmation bias, and the only defence is deliberate, systematic effort to seek out disconfirming evidence.

Figure 18.1 Six cognitive biases that commonly affect research thinking.

Confirmation bias is the most pervasive and dangerous of all cognitive biases. It is the tendency to seek out, notice, and remember information that confirms your existing beliefs while ignoring or dismissing information that contradicts them. Every researcher is susceptible to confirmation bias, and the only defence is deliberate, systematic effort to seek out disconfirming evidence.

Figure 18.1 Six cognitive biases that commonly affect research thinking.

Confirmation bias is the most pervasive and dangerous of all cognitive biases. It is the tendency to seek out, notice, and remember information that confirms your existing beliefs while ignoring or dismissing information that contradicts them. Every researcher is susceptible to confirmation bias, and the only defence is deliberate, systematic effort to seek out disconfirming evidence.

Cognitive biases are systematic patterns of deviation from rational judgement that affect everyone, including researchers. Confirmation bias - the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms existing beliefs - is perhaps the most pervasive and dangerous. A researcher with a strong expectation about the outcome of a study may unconsciously design the study in ways that make the expected result more likely, interpret ambiguous data in ways that favour the hypothesis, or give more attention to findings that confirm expectations while downplaying contradictory evidence (Kahneman, 2011).

Anchoring bias leads researchers to be disproportionately influenced by the first information they encounter. Availability bias causes overreliance on information that is easily recalled. Hindsight bias makes outcomes seem predictable after the fact, leading researchers to reconstruct their reasoning as more logical than it actually was.

18.3 Methodological and Social Biases

Methodological & Social Biases

📉 Selection Bias

Systematic differences between participants and non-participants.

😊 Social Desirability

Participants give answers they think are socially acceptable.

📋 Response Bias

Systematic patterns in how respondents answer questions.

Selection bias occurs when the sample is not representative of the population, leading to findings that cannot be generalised. Response bias occurs when participants answer questions in ways that do not reflect their true views - for example, due to social desirability. Publication bias is the tendency for journals to publish studies with statistically significant results while rejecting those with null or negative findings, leading to a distorted picture of the evidence base.

Social biases include gender bias, cultural bias, and institutional bias - systematic tendencies that privilege certain perspectives, populations, or approaches over others. These biases can affect which questions are considered important, which populations are studied, and whose voices are heard in the research process (Creswell & Creswell, 2018).

18.4 Mitigating Bias

While bias cannot be entirely eliminated, it can be substantially reduced through awareness, methodological safeguards, and transparency. Pre-registration of studies, blinding procedures, randomisation, triangulation, reflexive journals, and peer debriefing are all strategies that help detect and mitigate bias. The most important safeguard is intellectual honesty - the willingness to report findings as they are, even when they contradict expectations (Nosek et al., 2015).

Chapter Summary

Bias is a systematic distortion that can affect every stage of the research process. Cognitive biases, methodological biases, and social biases all threaten the validity of research findings. Awareness, methodological safeguards, and intellectual honesty are essential for detecting and mitigating bias.

Key Terms

Bias - A systematic tendency that produces research results that differ from the truth.

Confirmation bias - The tendency to seek and favour information that confirms existing beliefs.

Selection bias - Systematic error arising from a non-representative sample.

Publication bias - The tendency for journals to preferentially publish studies with significant positive results.

Review Questions

1. Explain the difference between bias and random error. Why is bias more dangerous?

2. Identify three cognitive biases that can affect research. How can each be mitigated?

3. What is publication bias, and how does it distort the research literature?

4. Design a study with built-in safeguards against at least two types of bias discussed in this chapter.

18.3 Methodological Biases

Beyond cognitive biases that affect individual researchers, there are systematic biases embedded in research methods and practices that can distort findings at a larger scale. Understanding these methodological biases is essential for designing rigorous studies and for interpreting published research critically.

Selection bias occurs when the participants in a study are not representative of the population to which the findings will be generalised. This can happen through self-selection (volunteers may differ from non-volunteers), convenience sampling (studying those who are most accessible rather than most representative), or attrition (participants who drop out of a study may differ from those who remain). Selection bias can lead to overestimation or underestimation of effects, depending on how the selected sample differs from the target population. (Heckman, 1979)

Publication bias is the tendency for studies with statistically significant results to be published more frequently than studies with null results. This creates a distorted picture of the evidence base, because the published literature overrepresents positive findings and underrepresents negative or inconclusive ones. Publication bias is a serious problem because it can lead to overconfidence in the effectiveness of interventions, drugs, or programmes that appear to work only because the studies showing they do not work were never published. (Rothstein, Sutton, & Borenstein, 2005)

Measurement bias occurs when the instruments or procedures used to collect data systematically distort the results. This can happen through poorly designed questionnaires, biased interview techniques, or instruments that have not been validated in the population being studied. For example, a depression scale developed and validated with Western adults may not accurately measure depression in non-Western cultures, where the experience and expression of depression may differ significantly.

18.4 Strategies for Minimising Bias

While bias can never be completely eliminated from research, there are numerous strategies for minimising its influence and mitigating its effects.

At the design stage, use randomisation wherever possible to reduce selection bias. Randomised assignment to treatment and control groups is the most effective way to ensure that observed differences are due to the treatment rather than to pre-existing differences between groups. When randomisation is not possible, use matching, stratification, or statistical control to reduce the influence of confounding variables.

At the data collection stage, use validated instruments that have been tested in populations similar to yours. Train data collectors to administer instruments consistently and without introducing systematic errors. Use blinding where appropriate - keeping participants and/or researchers unaware of group assignments to prevent expectations from influencing behaviour or measurement.

At the analysis stage, report all planned analyses, whether or not they produced significant results. Pre-register your hypotheses and analysis plan before collecting data, to prevent the temptation to adjust your analyses after seeing the results. And conduct sensitivity analyses to assess whether your conclusions change under different analytical assumptions.

At the reporting stage, be transparent about the limitations of your study and the potential biases that might have affected your findings. This transparency is not a weakness - it is a strength that increases the credibility of your work and allows readers to make informed judgements about the reliability of your conclusions.

18.5 Bias in the Research System

Beyond individual cognitive biases and methodological biases, there are systemic biases embedded in the research system itself that can distort the body of knowledge available to scholars and practitioners. Understanding these systemic biases is essential for interpreting the published literature accurately and for advocating reforms that would produce more reliable and representative knowledge.

Geographic bias refers to the overrepresentation of research from high-income, English-speaking countries in the global literature. Studies conducted in the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia dominate many fields, while research from Africa, Asia, and Latin America is underrepresented. This geographic bias means that the evidence base for many questions is skewed toward particular cultural, economic, and institutional contexts, and findings may not generalise to other parts of the world. (Sumathipala, Siribaddana, & Patel, 2004)

Language bias refers to the privileging of English-language publications in systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Research published in other languages is often excluded from these syntheses, which can distort the overall picture. For example, studies published in Chinese, Spanish, or Portuguese may contain important findings that are invisible to English-language reviewers. This bias is particularly concerning in fields like global health, education, and environmental science, where local knowledge and local evidence are critically important.

Funding bias refers to the influence of funding sources on the questions that are asked, the methods that are used, and the results that are reported. Industry-funded research is more likely to produce favourable results than independently funded research on the same topic, raising questions about the objectivity of commercially sponsored studies. While not all industry-funded research is biased, the pattern is consistent enough to warrant caution when evaluating studies funded by organisations with a commercial interest in the outcome.

Citation bias refers to the tendency for studies with positive or significant results to be cited more frequently than studies with null or negative results. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle in which positive findings become increasingly prominent while null findings fade from view. Over time, citation bias can create a distorted consensus that overestimates the effectiveness of interventions or the strength of relationships, because the studies that challenge this consensus are less visible and less cited. (Jannot, Agoritsas, Gayet-Ageron, & Perneger, 2013)

18.6 Practical Tools for Bias Reduction

While bias can never be eliminated entirely, several practical tools and procedures can significantly reduce its influence on your research.

Pre-registration involves publicly registering your hypotheses, methods, and analysis plan before collecting data. By committing to a specific analytical approach in advance, you reduce the temptation to modify your analyses after seeing the results - a practice known as p-hacking or HARKing (Hypothesising After the Results are Known). Pre-registration is increasingly expected by journals and funders, and it significantly enhances the credibility of your findings. (Nosek, Ebersole, DeHaven, & Mellor, 2018)

Triangulation involves using multiple methods, data sources, or investigators to study the same phenomenon. If different approaches converge on the same conclusion, you can be more confident that the finding is genuine rather than an artifact of a particular method or perspective. For example, combining survey data with interview data and observational data provides a richer and more trustworthy picture than any single method alone. (Denzin, 1978)

Peer debriefing involves regularly discussing your research with a colleague who is not involved in the project. The debriefer's role is to challenge your assumptions, question your interpretations, and identify potential biases that you may have overlooked. This external perspective is invaluable because it provides the kind of critical scrutiny that is very difficult to apply to your own work.

Audit trails involve maintaining detailed records of every decision you make during the research process - from question formulation to data collection to analysis to interpretation. These records allow others (and your future self) to retrace your reasoning and evaluate whether your decisions were justified. Audit trails are standard practice in qualitative research and are increasingly valued in quantitative research as well.

Negative case analysis involves deliberately seeking out cases that do not fit your emerging interpretation and examining them carefully. If your data analysis suggests that parental involvement improves student performance, look for cases where parental involvement was high but performance was low. These negative cases can reveal important nuances and limitations that strengthen rather than weaken your analysis.

chapters.title: Chapter 19: Reflexivity: Knowing How You Influence What You Find
chapters.content:
Chapter 19

Reflexivity: Knowing How You Influence What You Find

Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:
• Define reflexivity and explain its importance in research
• Distinguish between personal reflexivity and epistemological reflexivity
• Apply reflexive practices throughout the research process
• Maintain a reflexive journal to track your influence on the research
• Evaluate the role of reflexivity in enhancing research credibility

19.1 What Is Reflexivity?

Reflexivity is the practice of examining how your own characteristics, experiences, and assumptions influence your research. It recognises that the researcher is not a neutral instrument but an active participant in the knowledge-creation process, whose presence shapes what is observed, what is asked, and how findings are interpreted.

Reflexivity is the practice of examining how your own characteristics, experiences, and assumptions influence your research. It recognises that the researcher is not a neutral instrument but an active participant in the knowledge-creation process, whose presence shapes what is observed, what is asked, and how findings are interpreted.

Reflexivity is the practice of examining how your own characteristics, experiences, and assumptions influence your research. It recognises that the researcher is not a neutral instrument but an active participant in the knowledge-creation process, whose presence shapes what is observed, what is asked, and how findings are interpreted.

Reflexivity is the researcher's critical self-awareness of how their own background, values, assumptions, and position may influence the research process and its outcomes. It is the recognition that the researcher is not a neutral instrument but an active participant in the construction of knowledge - someone whose choices, interpretations, and perspectives inevitably shape the research (Denzin & Lincoln, 2018).

Reflexivity is particularly emphasised in qualitative research, where the researcher's role as the primary instrument of data collection and analysis makes their influence especially salient. However, reflexivity is equally relevant in quantitative research, where the researcher's choices about what to measure, how to operationalise variables, which statistical tests to use, and how to interpret results all reflect underlying assumptions and values.

19.2 Types of Reflexivity

Types of Reflexivity

🪞 Personal Reflexivity

How your values, experiences, and identity shape the research process and findings. (Finlay, 2002)

📐 Methodological Reflexivity

How your choice of methods influences what you can discover and what remains hidden.

🧠 Epistemological Reflexivity

How your assumptions about knowledge itself shape the questions you ask and answers you accept.

Figure 19.1 The three types of reflexivity and their interconnection.

Figure 19.1 The three types of reflexivity and their interconnection.

Figure 19.1 The three types of reflexivity and their interconnection.

Personal reflexivity involves examining how your own identity - your gender, race, class, nationality, disciplinary training, and personal experiences - shapes your research. A researcher studying poverty who has never experienced economic hardship will bring a different perspective than one who has. Neither perspective is inherently superior, but awareness of these differences is essential for producing credible, honest research.

Epistemological reflexivity involves examining how the research design itself shapes the knowledge produced. Every methodological choice - from the research paradigm to the sampling strategy to the analytical technique - enables certain findings while foreclosing others. Epistemological reflexivity asks: How has my approach to this study influenced what I can and cannot find? What alternative approaches might have produced different insights? (Creswell & Creswell, 2018).

19.3 Practising Reflexivity: The Reflexive Journal

A reflexive journal is a personal document in which the researcher records their thoughts, feelings, decisions, and observations throughout the research process. It serves as a tool for tracking how the researcher's perspectives evolve over time, how their presence may be affecting participants, and how their analytical decisions are being made. Regular journal entries - written after interviews, during data analysis, and at key decision points - provide a transparent record that enhances the credibility and trustworthiness of the research (Merriam & Tisdell, 2016).

Chapter Summary

Reflexivity is the researcher's critical self-awareness of their influence on the research process. Personal reflexivity examines the researcher's identity and experiences; epistemological reflexivity examines how methodological choices shape knowledge. A reflexive journal provides a practical tool for maintaining ongoing self-awareness throughout the study.

Key Terms

Reflexivity - Critical self-awareness of how the researcher's background, values, and choices influence the research.

Personal reflexivity - Examination of how the researcher's identity and experiences shape their research perspective.

Epistemological reflexivity - Examination of how research design and methodology shape the knowledge produced.

Reflexive journal - A personal document for recording the researcher's thoughts, decisions, and evolving perspectives.

Review Questions

1. Define reflexivity and explain why it is important for all researchers, not just qualitative researchers.

2. Distinguish between personal and epistemological reflexivity. Provide an example of each.

3. How does a reflexive journal enhance the credibility of research? What should it contain?

19.3 Practising Reflexivity

Reflexivity is not a one-time exercise to be completed at the beginning of your project and then forgotten. It is an ongoing practice that should inform every stage of your research, from question formulation to data collection to interpretation and writing. The reflexive researcher continually asks: How are my characteristics, experiences, and assumptions influencing what I am seeing, what I am asking, and what I am concluding?

One practical tool for practising reflexivity is the reflective journal. This is a separate document from your research notes in which you record your thoughts, feelings, and reactions as you conduct your research. After each interview, observation session, or data analysis session, spend a few minutes writing about what surprised you, what troubled you, what confirmed your expectations, and what challenged them. Over time, patterns will emerge in your journal that reveal your biases, preferences, and blind spots. (Ortlipp, 2008)

Another tool is peer debriefing. Share your emerging findings and interpretations with a trusted colleague and ask them to challenge your assumptions. A good peer debriefer will ask difficult questions: Why did you interpret that data point this way rather than that way? What alternative explanation have you not considered? How might a researcher from a different background interpret the same data? These questions are not attacks - they are gifts that help you see what you cannot see alone. (Lincoln & Guba, 1985)

A third tool is member checking - sharing your findings with your research participants and asking whether your interpretation resonates with their experience. This is particularly valuable in qualitative research, where the researcher's interpretation plays a central role. Participants may confirm your interpretation, offer nuances you had missed, or challenge aspects of your analysis that do not ring true. Their feedback enriches your understanding and increases the credibility of your findings. (Birt, Scott, Cavers, Campbell, & Walter, 2016)

19.4 Reflexivity Across Research Paradigms

The role and practice of reflexivity vary across different research paradigms. In qualitative research, reflexivity is often considered a core methodological requirement. The researcher is recognised as the primary instrument of data collection and analysis, and their subjectivity is acknowledged as an unavoidable influence on the research process. Reflexive practices such as journaling, member checking, and audit trails are standard features of rigorous qualitative research.

In quantitative research, reflexivity has traditionally received less attention, partly because the emphasis on standardised methods and statistical analysis can create an illusion of objectivity. However, even quantitative researchers make subjective decisions - about which variables to measure, which instruments to use, which analyses to conduct, and how to interpret results. Increasingly, quantitative researchers are recognising the importance of reflexivity in these decisions and are finding ways to incorporate reflexive practices into their work.

In mixed-methods research, reflexivity is particularly important because the researcher must navigate between different epistemological positions - the objectivist assumptions of quantitative research and the constructivist assumptions of qualitative research. Being reflexive about how you move between these positions, and about how your own preferences and training influence the integration of quantitative and qualitative findings, is essential for producing coherent and credible mixed-methods research.

19.5 Writing Reflexively

Writing reflexively means incorporating your reflections on your own role and influence into your research report. This is not about inserting autobiography into your thesis - it is about being transparent with your readers about how your characteristics, experiences, and assumptions may have shaped your research. This transparency enhances the credibility of your work by allowing readers to evaluate the influence of the researcher on the research.

In qualitative research, reflexive writing typically appears in the methodology section, where you describe your positionality - your relationship to the research topic, the participants, and the research context. You might discuss your cultural background, your professional experience, your prior beliefs about the topic, and how these factors may have influenced your data collection and interpretation. This is not about confessing bias; it is about providing readers with the information they need to assess how your perspective may have shaped your findings.

Reflexive writing can also appear in the discussion section, where you consider how your findings might have been different if a researcher with a different background had conducted the study. Would a male researcher have elicited different responses from female participants? Would a researcher from outside the community have noticed different patterns? These questions do not undermine your findings; they contextualise them, making clear that they represent one perspective among several possible ones.

In quantitative research, reflexive writing is less common but increasingly recognised as valuable. You might reflect on why you chose certain variables to measure and not others, why you selected a particular analytical approach, and how your theoretical assumptions influenced your interpretation of the results. While quantitative methods are often presented as objective and researcher-independent, the reality is that numerous subjective decisions shape every quantitative study, and being transparent about these decisions strengthens rather than weakens your work.

19.6 Reflexivity and Power

A critical dimension of reflexivity involves examining the power dynamics inherent in the research relationship. Researchers typically hold more power than their participants - they control the research agenda, decide which questions to ask, determine how data is interpreted, and choose what to publish. This power imbalance has implications for the ethics, validity, and impact of your research.

Power dynamics are particularly pronounced when research involves vulnerable or marginalised populations. When a university-educated researcher studies the experiences of people living in poverty, or when a researcher from the global North studies communities in the global South, the power differential can influence every aspect of the research process - from whether participants feel able to refuse participation, to whether they feel comfortable expressing views that differ from what they perceive the researcher wants to hear.

Reflexive awareness of power dynamics does not mean you should avoid studying populations that differ from you - important research would be impossible if every researcher studied only people like themselves. But it does mean that you should be thoughtful about how power differences might affect your data, transparent about these dynamics in your research report, and committed to practices that minimise the negative effects of power imbalance.

Participatory research approaches offer one way to address power dynamics by involving community members as co-researchers rather than passive subjects. In participatory action research, for example, community members help define the research questions, participate in data collection and analysis, and share in the dissemination of findings. While participatory approaches are not appropriate for all research questions, they can produce more equitable and more valid research by drawing on the expertise of those who are most directly affected by the issues being studied. (Reason & Bradbury, 2008)

19.7 Reflexivity as a Habit of Mind

The ultimate goal of reflexivity is not just to complete a reflexive section in your methodology chapter. It is to develop reflexivity as a habit of mind - an ongoing practice of self-awareness and critical self-examination that informs everything you do as a researcher.

As a habit of mind, reflexivity means continuously asking yourself: How might my perspective be influencing what I see and what I miss?
